GK-CAN-001.name: Canna
GK-CAN-001.subtitle: Tropical foliage and flower impact for warm sheltered spots.

quickCare.Water: Water freely in growth; keep moist but not waterlogged
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered from wind
quickCare.Feeding: Fortnightly high-potash feed once flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Cut stems to 15cm after first frost blackens foliage

GK-CAN-001.notes: Not hardy below roughly -2C. Lift and store rhizomes over winter in cold gardens, or grow in a pot and move under cover.
GK-CAN-001.soil: Rich, moisture-retentive soil with good structure.
GK-CAN-001.medium: Peat-free compost enriched with well-rotted manure or garden compost.

seasonalActions.Spring: Start rhizomes into growth under cover from March; plant out after last frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Feed fortnightly and deadhead spent flowers.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back after blackened by frost; lift tender types.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store rhizomes frost-free and just barely moist, or protect in-ground with a thick mulch in mild areas.

problems.name: Slugs and snails
problems.fix: Shred young shoots; use barriers or torchlight removal early in the season.

problems.name: Canna virus (streaking/mottling)
problems.fix: No cure; discard and buy certified virus-free stock rather than treating.

problems.name: Poor flowering
problems.fix: Usually not enough sun or feed; move to a brighter spot and start a potash feed.

propagation.season: Divide dormant rhizomes in early spring before new growth starts.
propagation.methods: Divide rhizomes so each piece has at least one growing eye.
propagation.tips: Let cut surfaces callus for a day before potting to reduce rot risk.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mainly ornamental; some varieties attract insects but value varies.

timeline.Pruning: Cut down after frost; overwinter rhizomes

gallery: Seed pod, fruit, tuber or spent flower if applicable

GK-CAN-001.weeklyFact: Canna's huge paddle-shaped leaves are close relatives of the banana and ginger families, which is part of why the plant looks so tropical.
GK-CAN-001.pollinatorFact: Canna flowers are bold and tropical-looking; value depends on variety and whether insects can access the flower parts.
weeklyFacts: Canna's huge paddle-shaped leaves are close relatives of the banana and ginger families, which is part of why the plant looks so tropical.
Canna rhizomes were traditionally ground into starch by some Andean cultures long before they became a garden ornamental.

GK-CAR-001.name: Carrot
GK-CAR-001.subtitle: Sweet roots for deep pots and fine soil.

quickCare.Water: Keep steady, not soggy
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Avoid rich nitrogen feed
quickCare.Pruning: Thin seedlings carefully

GK-CAR-001.notes: Carrots are a good test for soil and medium notes because the root tells you exactly how good the growing conditions were.
GK-CAR-001.soil: Stone-free, light soil. Heavy or stony soil can fork the roots.
GK-CAR-001.medium: Fine compost mixed with sand or grit for containers. Avoid fresh manure.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow thinly once soil warms.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells and thin carefully.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Harvest as needed.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted roots or protect late crops.

problems.name: Forked roots
problems.fix: Usually caused by stones, compacted soil or rich fresh manure. Use a finer medium next time.

problems.name: Carrot fly
problems.fix: Cover with fine mesh and avoid bruising foliage when thinning.

propagation.season: Spring to early summer for most home sowings.
propagation.methods: Sow seed directly where it is to grow.
propagation.tips: Do not transplant carrots unless using special short-root types. They hate root disturbance.
propagation.tracker: Track sowing date, thinning date and first harvest.

pollinator.value: Carrot flowers are useful to insects if allowed to flower in the second year, but most crops are harvested before then.

GK-CAR-001.pollinatorFact: Umbellifer flowers like carrot relatives can be brilliant landing pads for hoverflies and other beneficial insects.

timeline.Pruning: Thin seedlings

GK-CAR-001.weeklyFact: Carrot seed is tiny, which is why many gardeners accidentally sow enough carrots for a small village.
weeklyFacts: Carrot seed is tiny, which is why many gardeners accidentally sow enough carrots for a small village.
Wild carrot root is naturally pale and stringy — the plump orange carrot we know today was selectively bred in the Netherlands.

GK-COU-001.name: Courgette
GK-COU-001.subtitle: Big leaves, big flowers and regular summer picking.

quickCare.Water: Water deeply and often
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Feed when flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Remove damaged leaves

GK-COU-001.notes: Courgettes are great for testing harvest tracking because fruit can go from perfect to comedy size very quickly.
GK-COU-001.medium: Peat-free compost with garden compost or manure-based improver in a large container.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in pots.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water, feed and harvest small fruit regularly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Remove tired plants after cropping.
seasonalActions.Winter: Plan next year and compost healthy remains.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Common later in the season. Water the soil not the leaves, improve airflow and remove badly affected leaves.

problems.name: Poor fruit set
problems.fix: Often pollination or weather related. Encourage pollinators and try hand pollination if needed.

propagation.season: Sow in spring, plant out after frost risk.
propagation.methods: Seed in pots; plant out carefully without disturbing roots.
propagation.tips: Start with one or two plants unless you are emotionally prepared for courgette season.
propagation.tracker: Track sowing, first flower, first harvest and weekly harvest count.

pollinator.value: Large yellow flowers attract pollinators, and good pollination improves fruit set.

GK-COU-001.pollinatorFact: Courgettes have male and female flowers, and bees do a lot of the matchmaking.

timeline.Pruning: Remove damaged leaves

GK-COU-001.weeklyFact: A courgette can hide under one leaf and become a marrow while you are making a cup of tea.
weeklyFacts: A courgette can hide under one leaf and become a marrow while you are making a cup of tea.
Courgettes and pumpkins are the same species, Cucurbita pepo, just bred and picked at very different stages.

GK-DAH-001.name: Dahlia
GK-DAH-001.subtitle: Bold late-summer colour from tubers.

quickCare.Water: Water well in dry spells once established
quickCare.Light: Full sun, at least 6 hours a day
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly high-potash feed once buds form
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead constantly; pinch young plants to bush out

GK-DAH-001.notes: Stake early — dahlias put on height fast and snap in wind once top-heavy with flower.
GK-DAH-001.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter.
GK-DAH-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers; needs regular feeding as it's hungry.

seasonalActions.Spring: Pot up tubers under cover from March; plant out after last frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead weekly, feed and stake as growth continues.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Lift tubers after the first frost blackens foliage, or mulch heavily to overwinter in mild, free-draining gardens.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted tubers somewhere frost-free, dark and just barely moist.

problems.name: Earwigs
problems.fix: Chew petal edges at night. Trap in upturned straw-filled pots on canes, empty in the morning.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Worse late season; improve airflow and water at the base, not the leaves.

problems.name: Slugs on young shoots
problems.fix: Protect new spring growth with barriers or torchlight removal.

propagation.season: Divide dormant tubers in spring, just as eyes start to show.
propagation.methods: Divide tuber clumps so each division has a piece of the old stem base with at least one eye.
propagation.tips: Basal cuttings taken from sprouted tubers in early spring multiply stock fastest.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open-centred dahlias are better for pollinators than very double forms.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead summer; lift tubers after frost

gallery: Seed pod, fruit, tuber or spent flower if applicable

GK-DAH-001.weeklyFact: Dahlias were originally grown by the Aztecs as a food crop for their edible tubers, long before Europeans bred them into today's flamboyant flowers.
GK-DAH-001.pollinatorFact: Open-centred dahlias are much better for bees than tight decorative doubles, which can hide the pollen and nectar.
weeklyFacts: Dahlias were originally grown by the Aztecs as a food crop for their edible tubers, long before Europeans bred them into today's flamboyant flowers.
There are over 40 recognised dahlia flower forms, from tight little pompons to dinner-plate-sized blooms.

GK-FRN-001.name: Fern
GK-FRN-001.subtitle: Shade-loving foliage for calm, green corners.

quickCare.Water: Keep lightly moist
quickCare.Light: Shade to indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in spring
quickCare.Pruning: Remove dead fronds

GK-FRN-001.notes: Ferns prefer consistent moisture and shelter from drying winds. Avoid strong afternoon sun.
GK-FRN-001.soil: Humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil with good structure.
GK-FRN-001.medium: Peat-free multipurpose compost mixed with leaf mould or fine bark for moisture retention.

seasonalActions.Spring: Remove old fronds as new growth appears.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water during dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Top dress with leaf mould.
seasonalActions.Winter: Tidy dead fronds when the plant is dormant or growth has slowed, usually November to March. Leaving some cover can help protect the crown.

problems.name: Crispy fronds
problems.fix: Increase humidity/moisture and move from direct sun.

problems.name: Slug damage
problems.fix: Check under rims and use barriers/traps rather than harsh chemicals.

propagation.season: Spring division.
propagation.methods: Divide established clumps or grow from spores for advanced propagation.
propagation.tips: Keep divisions moist until re-established.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mainly habitat and shelter value rather than nectar value.

timeline.Pruning: Mar-Apr

photoPlan.hero: Fresh fronds with shaded pot/garden context.

GK-FRN-001.weeklyFact: Ferns are ancient plants that reproduce by spores rather than flowers — very calm, very green, very Jurassic.
GK-FRN-001.pollinatorFact: Ferns do not provide flowers for pollinators, but they do create shelter and humidity for small garden life.
weeklyFacts: Ferns are ancient plants that reproduce by spores rather than flowers — very calm, very green, very Jurassic.
Fern fossils date back over 350 million years, making them older than most trees and even the dinosaurs.

GK-HOS-001.name: Hosta
GK-HOS-001.subtitle: Bold shade foliage that slugs love just as much as you do.

quickCare.Water: Keep consistently moist, never let dry out fully
quickCare.Light: Part to full shade; morning sun is fine, avoid midday sun
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced liquid feed monthly through spring and summer
quickCare.Pruning: Cut all foliage to the ground once it dies back in autumn

GK-HOS-001.notes: The biggest threat to hostas is slugs and snails, not disease — protect emerging shoots in spring above anything else.
GK-HOS-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould or well-rotted organic matter for moisture retention.

seasonalActions.Spring: Protect emerging shoots from slugs immediately — this is the most vulnerable stage.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells and keep feeding monthly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back foliage once it yellows and collapses after first frosts.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant and fully hardy; no protection needed outdoors.

problems.name: Slugs and snails
problems.fix: The main hosta problem. Use copper tape, grit barriers, beer traps or torchlight removal at night, especially on new spring shoots.

problems.name: Sun scorch
problems.fix: Pale, crispy leaf edges mean too much direct sun — move to deeper shade.

problems.name: Virus (mottled/streaked leaves)
problems.fix: No cure; remove and destroy affected plants rather than propagating from them.

propagation.season: Divide dormant clumps in early spring or autumn.
propagation.methods: Lift the whole clump and divide with a spade or knife, keeping several growth points per division.
propagation.tips: Water divisions in well and keep moist until re-established.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Tubular summer flowers are visited by bees and occasionally hummingbird-mimicking hawkmoths at dusk.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back Oct-Nov after die-back

gallery: Slug damage if present, for reference

GK-HOS-001.weeklyFact: Some hosta leaves can grow large enough to hold a full cup of rainwater after a summer shower.
GK-HOS-001.pollinatorFact: Hosta's tubular flowers are shaped for bees and long-tongued pollinators, and are sometimes visited by moths at dusk.
weeklyFacts: Some hosta leaves can grow large enough to hold a full cup of rainwater after a summer shower.
Hostas were originally bred and prized in Japan for centuries before becoming a Western shade-garden staple.

GK-HYD-001.name: Hydrangea
GK-HYD-001.subtitle: Big summer flowerheads that shift colour with soil pH.

quickCare.Water: Water generously, especially in pots — wilts fast when dry
quickCare.Light: Morning sun, afternoon shade ideal
quickCare.Feeding: Spring feed with an ericaceous formula if you want blue flowers
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flowerheads to the first strong bud pair in spring, not autumn

GK-HYD-001.notes: For blue flowers the soil needs to be acidic with available aluminium; for pink, more alkaline. White varieties don't change colour regardless of soil.
GK-HYD-001.soil: Moist, fertile, humus-rich soil. Acidic soil produces blue flowers on macrophylla types; alkaline produces pink.
GK-HYD-001.medium: Ericaceous peat-free compost for pots if aiming for blue flowers; standard peat-free compost for pink or white.

seasonalActions.Spring: Prune out old flowerheads to the first healthy bud pair once new growth is visible.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water generously, especially in containers, and deadhead as flowers fade.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave old flowerheads on over winter — they protect next year's buds from frost.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave faded flowerheads in place for frost protection; do not prune now.

problems.name: Wilting in pots
problems.fix: Hydrangeas in containers dry out fast in summer heat; water daily in hot spells or move to part shade.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Usually pruned at the wrong time (cutting off next year's buds) or frost damage to buds — check pruning timing.

problems.name: Leaf scorch
problems.fix: Too much direct afternoon sun; move to a spot with afternoon shade.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer.
propagation.methods: Take non-flowering shoot tips, remove lower leaves, root in a free-draining cutting mix.
propagation.tips: Keep cuttings humid (a clear bag works) until rooted, then pot on individually.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mophead varieties are mostly sterile ornamental flowers; lacecap types offer more accessible pollen for insects.

timeline.Pruning: Mar-Apr, to first bud pair

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Hydrangea in flower.
gallery: Colour comparison if soil pH varies across the garden

GK-HYD-001.weeklyFact: A hydrangea's flower colour is a live pH test for your soil — blue means acidic, pink means alkaline.
GK-HYD-001.pollinatorFact: Lacecap hydrangeas have small fertile flowers around the rim that are far more useful to pollinators than the showy sterile mophead types.
weeklyFacts: A hydrangea's flower colour is a live pH test for your soil — blue means acidic, pink means alkaline.
The 'macrophylla' in Hydrangea macrophylla simply means 'large-leaved' in Latin.

GK-ACE-001.name: Japanese Maple
GK-ACE-001.subtitle: Elegant, slow-growing tree prized for foliage and autumn colour.

quickCare.Water: Keep evenly moist, especially in pots — never let it dry out or waterlog
quickCare.Light: Dappled or part shade, sheltered from strong wind and hot midday sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light, slow-release feed in spring only — avoid high nitrogen
quickCare.Pruning: Minimal — remove dead or crossing branches in late autumn/winter while dormant

GK-ACE-001.notes: Leaf edges scorch easily in strong wind, hot sun or drought — shelter is as important as soil.
GK-ACE-001.soil: Humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil.
GK-ACE-001.medium: Peat-free ericaceous or acid-friendly compost for pots, with excellent drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Watch for late frost damage on new leaves; feed lightly once.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and shelter from scorching sun and wind.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Enjoy the colour change; keep roots moist as leaves turn.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune dead or crossing wood only while fully dormant; protect pots from freezing solid.

problems.name: Leaf scorch
problems.fix: Brown, crispy leaf edges from wind, sun or drought — move to a more sheltered spot and water consistently.

problems.name: Verticillium wilt
problems.fix: Sudden branch dieback; remove affected wood promptly and avoid replanting maples in the same spot.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer, or grafting for named cultivars.
propagation.methods: Named varieties are usually grafted; species types can be grown from seed sown fresh in autumn.
propagation.tips: Seed-grown maples won't come true to a named cultivar — expect variation.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small spring flowers offer modest early-season nectar and pollen for insects before the canopy is fully out.

timeline.Seeding: Sow fresh seed in autumn if species type
timeline.Pruning: Nov-Jan while dormant

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Japanese Maple, ideally showing canopy shape.

GK-ACE-001.weeklyFact: Japanese maples can live for well over a century and are often trained as bonsai for exactly that reason.
GK-ACE-001.pollinatorFact: The small red-green spring flowers are easy to miss but provide early nectar before many other plants are in bloom.
weeklyFacts: Japanese maples can live for well over a century and are often trained as bonsai for exactly that reason.
The word 'palmatum' in Acer palmatum refers to the leaf shape, which fans out like an open hand.

GK-LAV-001.name: Lavender
GK-LAV-001.subtitle: Scented, sun-loving, pollinator friendly.

quickCare.Water: Lightly once soil is dry
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered spot
quickCare.Feeding: Minimal feeding needed
quickCare.Pruning: Trim after flowering

GK-LAV-001.notes: Do not overwater. Lavender dislikes sitting in wet compost. Add grit or perlite if the potting mix is too rich or heavy.
GK-LAV-001.soil: Free-draining, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral soil. Avoid heavy wet compost.
GK-LAV-001.medium: Peat-free compost mixed with horticultural grit or perlite. For pots, aim for sharp drainage and raise the pot slightly in winter.

seasonalActions.Spring: Check drainage, remove dead growth and lightly refresh top dressing.
seasonalActions.Summer: Trim lightly after flowering and avoid heavy feeding.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering and keep the crown dry.
seasonalActions.Winter: Keep it on the dry side and avoid waterlogged compost. If pruning is needed, wait until the plant is dormant or growth is quiet — usually November to March — but avoid cutting hard into old woody stems.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: Usually caused by wet compost. Improve drainage, reduce watering and consider repotting into a gritty mix.

problems.name: Poor flowering
problems.fix: Move to stronger sun and avoid rich feeding.

problems.name: Aphids
problems.fix: Squash by hand or wash off with water; encourage hoverflies and ladybirds.

propagation.season: Late summer for semi-ripe cuttings; spring for seed.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings root well in a gritty, free-draining medium.
propagation.tips: Avoid overly humid sealed conditions; gentle ventilation helps reduce rot.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Excellent nectar source for bees, butterflies and hoverflies. Flowering lavender can support pollinators while ornamentals nearby provide colour and structure.

timeline.Pruning: Aug-Sep

photoPlan.hero: Whole plant in flower, ideally with bees visible.

GK-LAV-001.weeklyFact: Lavender has been used for centuries in gardens, medicine cupboards and linen drawers — basically the original plant-based air freshener.
GK-LAV-001.pollinatorFact: Open lavender flowers are brilliant for bees because the nectar is easy to access and the plant flowers over a useful summer window.
weeklyFacts: Lavender has been used for centuries in gardens, medicine cupboards and linen drawers — basically the original plant-based air freshener.
The name lavender comes from the Latin 'lavare,' meaning 'to wash,' since Romans scented their bathwater with it.

GK-LET-001.name: Lettuce
GK-LET-001.subtitle: Fast, useful salad leaves for pots and beds.

quickCare.Water: Keep evenly moist
quickCare.Light: Sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed if growth slows
quickCare.Pruning: Pick outer leaves regularly

GK-LET-001.notes: Lettuce is a good tester plant because it changes quickly. It shows whether GardenKey is useful for repeat jobs like watering, harvesting and sowing follow-up batches.
GK-LET-001.soil: Moist, fertile, free-draining soil. Avoid letting containers dry out completely.
GK-LET-001.medium: Peat-free multipurpose compost with a little fine compost or worm cast for moisture holding.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow small batches every 2–3 weeks.
seasonalActions.Summer: Give light shade in hot spells and harvest regularly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Sow hardy varieties under cover.
seasonalActions.Winter: Growth slows; protect from harsh weather and wet compost.

problems.name: Bolting
problems.fix: Heat or stress can make lettuce run to seed. Sow little and often, keep evenly moist and use shade in hot weather.

problems.name: Slugs
problems.fix: Use barriers, night checks and tidy hiding places. Avoid pellets where pets/wildlife may be affected.

propagation.season: Spring to autumn, depending on variety.
propagation.methods: Sow seed thinly in trays, modules or directly in containers.
propagation.tips: Label each sowing date. Small batches are better than one huge glut.
propagation.tracker: Easy future tracker: sowing date, first pick date, flavour and whether it bolted.

pollinator.value: Lettuce is mainly grown for leaves, not pollinators, but leaving one plant to flower can feed small insects.

GK-LET-001.pollinatorFact: If one lettuce bolts, leave it briefly and watch how many tiny insects visit the flowers.

timeline.Pruning: Pick outer leaves as needed

GK-LET-001.weeklyFact: Lettuce is one of the best plants for testing a garden app because you can see results in weeks, not years.
weeklyFacts: Lettuce is one of the best plants for testing a garden app because you can see results in weeks, not years.
Ancient Egyptians grew lettuce not primarily for salad but for the oil pressed from its seeds.

GK-POT-001.name: Potato
GK-POT-001.subtitle: A satisfying crop for bags, tubs and beds.

quickCare.Water: Water well once growing
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Feed lightly in containers
quickCare.Pruning: Earth up stems

GK-POT-001.notes: Potatoes are good for beginners because the harvest feels like treasure hunting.
GK-POT-001.medium: Peat-free compost mixed with garden compost in a deep bag or large tub.

seasonalActions.Spring: Plant chitted seed potatoes.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water well, earth up and harvest earlies.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Lift maincrop before prolonged wet.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store in a cool dark place.

problems.name: Blight
problems.fix: Remove affected foliage and avoid wet leaves. Choose resistant varieties where blight is common.

problems.name: Scab
problems.fix: Often worse in dry or alkaline conditions. Keep moisture steady while tubers form.

propagation.season: Plant in spring.
propagation.methods: Seed potatoes planted in bags, tubs or beds.
propagation.tips: Do not use supermarket potatoes for trials if you want clean, predictable results.
propagation.tracker: Track planting date, earthing-up dates and harvest weight.

pollinator.value: Potato flowers can attract insects, but most value is food production rather than ornamental pollinator support.

GK-POT-001.pollinatorFact: Potato flowers are not the main reason to grow them for wildlife, but they can still bring insect visitors.

timeline.Pruning: Earth up; remove blighted foliage

GK-POT-001.weeklyFact: Potatoes are related to tomatoes, which explains why potato flowers can look strangely familiar.
weeklyFacts: Potatoes are related to tomatoes, which explains why potato flowers can look strangely familiar.
Potatoes were the first vegetable ever grown in space, as part of a 1995 NASA and University of Wisconsin experiment.

GK-ROS-001.name: Rose
GK-ROS-001.subtitle: Classic flowering shrub with high ornamental value.

quickCare.Water: Deeply in dry spells
quickCare.Light: Full sun preferred
quickCare.Feeding: Feed in spring and early summer
quickCare.Pruning: Prune in late winter

GK-ROS-001.notes: Deadhead regularly to encourage flowering. Keep air moving around the plant to reduce fungal disease.
GK-ROS-001.soil: Fertile, moisture-retentive but free-draining soil. Avoid waterlogging.
GK-ROS-001.medium: For pots, use a loam-based peat-free compost with added organic matter and good drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Feed and mulch; check new shoots for aphids.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead, water deeply in dry periods and watch for blackspot.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Clear fallen diseased leaves.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune when dormant, usually November to March. Aim for an open shape, remove dead or crossing stems, and use clean secateurs.

problems.name: Blackspot
problems.fix: Remove affected leaves, avoid overhead watering and improve airflow.

problems.name: Aphids
problems.fix: Wipe off early colonies or spray with water; avoid broad insecticides where possible.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Water consistently, improve airflow and avoid drought stress.

propagation.season: Hardwood cuttings in autumn or winter.
propagation.methods: Take pencil-thick cuttings from healthy stems and insert into gritty compost.
propagation.tips: Label varieties carefully; many roses look similar when dormant.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Single and semi-double roses are usually better for pollinators than very double flowers.

timeline.Pruning: Feb-Mar

photoPlan.hero: Open flower and whole shrub context.

GK-ROS-001.weeklyFact: Open-centred roses can provide pollen access for bees, unlike many very double varieties.
GK-ROS-001.pollinatorFact: Single and semi-double roses are usually more useful to pollinators than very full double roses, because insects can actually reach the centre.
weeklyFacts: Open-centred roses can provide pollen access for bees, unlike many very double varieties.
The rose family also includes apples, cherries, strawberries and almonds — a surprisingly wide-ranging relative list.

GK-SAL-001.name: Salvia
GK-SAL-001.subtitle: Spiky, long-flowering perennial that bees can't get enough of.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water in prolonged dry spells only
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed — too rich a diet causes floppy growth
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flower spikes to encourage a second flush; cut back hard in spring

GK-SAL-001.notes: Deadheading spent spikes is the single biggest thing you can do to keep salvia flowering all summer.
GK-SAL-001.soil: Free-draining soil; tolerates poor soil better than rich, wet ground.
GK-SAL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for sharp drainage, especially in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut old growth right back to fresh shoots at the base.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent spikes regularly to prolong flowering into autumn.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave the last flush of growth in place over winter for frost protection.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave old stems standing — cutting back now exposes the crown to frost damage.

problems.name: Floppy growth
problems.fix: Usually too much shade or too rich a feed; move to full sun and ease off feeding.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Improve airflow and avoid overhead watering in humid weather.

problems.name: Winter losses in wet soil
problems.fix: Poor drainage is the main killer, not cold. Improve drainage or grow in pots in heavy clay gardens.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in late spring, or division in spring.
propagation.methods: Take non-flowering shoot tips for cuttings, or divide established clumps.
propagation.tips: Cuttings root quickly in a free-draining mix on a warm windowsill.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: One of the best border plants for bees — long tubular flowers over a very long season.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead through summer; cut back hard in spring

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Salvia in flower.
gallery: Bees or pollinators visiting if captured

GK-SAL-001.weeklyFact: Salvia's tubular flowers are shaped almost exactly for a bumblebee's tongue length.
GK-SAL-001.pollinatorFact: Salvia is consistently one of the top-rated plants in pollinator surveys thanks to its long flowering season and easy nectar access.
weeklyFacts: Salvia's tubular flowers are shaped almost exactly for a bumblebee's tongue length.
The genus Salvia is the largest in the mint family, with somewhere around 900 recognised species.

GK-GLA-001.name: Gladiolus
GK-GLA-001.subtitle: Tall, dramatic flower spikes for late summer borders and cutting.

quickCare.Water: Water well during the growing season, especially as spikes form
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered from strong wind
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed at planting, then high-potash as buds form
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flower spikes; cut foliage down after it yellows in autumn

GK-GLA-001.notes: Stake tall varieties — the flower spikes are heavy and catch wind easily.
GK-GLA-001.medium: Peat-free compost improved with garden compost; good drainage is essential in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Plant corms from mid-spring once frost risk has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water regularly and stake as spikes develop.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut flowers for the house as they open; lift corms after foliage yellows in cold gardens.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted corms somewhere cool, dry and frost-free.

problems.name: Thrips
problems.fix: Fine silvery streaking on leaves and flowers — remove affected foliage and encourage natural predators like lacewings.

problems.name: Corm rot in storage
problems.fix: Usually from storing damp corms; dry thoroughly before storing and discard any soft ones.

propagation.season: Divide cormlets from the base of lifted corms in spring.
propagation.methods: Small cormlets can be grown on for a couple of seasons before they flower.
propagation.tips: Label varieties carefully — corms look near-identical when lifted.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mostly grown for cut-flower impact rather than pollinator value; the tightly packed spikes offer limited access for insects.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back after foliage yellows

gallery: Whole spike in a border setting

GK-GLA-001.weeklyFact: Gladiolus corms are technically called corms, not bulbs — they're solid stem tissue rather than layered scales.
GK-GLA-001.pollinatorFact: Gladiolus spikes are showy but the tightly packed florets make nectar and pollen harder for insects to reach than more open flower shapes.
weeklyFacts: Gladiolus corms are technically called corms, not bulbs — they're solid stem tissue rather than layered scales.
The name Gladiolus comes from the Latin for 'little sword,' describing the shape of the leaves.

GK-TUL-001.name: Tulip
GK-TUL-001.subtitle: Classic spring bulb for bold, reliable colour.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting, then rely on rainfall — avoid summer watering while dormant
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Feed lightly with high-potash as leaves emerge in spring
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering; leave foliage to die back naturally

GK-TUL-001.notes: Deadhead but don't cut foliage early — the leaves are recharging the bulb for next year.
GK-TUL-001.soil: Free-draining soil; tulips dislike sitting wet over summer.
GK-TUL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Foliage emerges and flowers open; deadhead as blooms fade.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back naturally — leave it, don't tidy early.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Plant new bulbs from October into cold, free-draining soil.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bulbs are dormant underground; no care needed.

problems.name: Tulip fire (botrytis)
problems.fix: Distorted, scorched-looking shoots — remove and destroy affected bulbs, don't replant tulips in the same spot for a few years.

problems.name: Squirrels digging up bulbs
problems.fix: Cover freshly planted areas with mesh until shoots appear.

propagation.season: Grown from bulbs, not typically propagated at home.
propagation.methods: Bulbs naturally produce small offset bulbs over years.
propagation.tips: Lift and store if you want guaranteed colour-matched displays next year, as many modern tulips don't reliably repeat.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open, cup-shaped flowers give bees reasonable access, especially on warmer days when flowers open fully.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering

gallery: Bed or container in full bloom

GK-TUL-001.weeklyFact: Tulip bulbs were once so valuable in 17th-century Holland that a single rare bulb could cost more than a house.
GK-TUL-001.pollinatorFact: Tulips open wide on sunny days, giving early-season bees good access to pollen when few other flowers are out yet.
weeklyFacts: Tulip bulbs were once so valuable in 17th-century Holland that a single rare bulb could cost more than a house.
Tulips didn't originate in the Netherlands at all — they're native to Central Asia and were first cultivated in the Ottoman Empire.

GK-SUN-001.name: Sunflower
GK-SUN-001.subtitle: Fast, cheerful annual that tracks the sun and feeds the birds.

quickCare.Water: Water generously, especially once flower heads form
quickCare.Light: Full sun, at least 6 hours a day
quickCare.Feeding: Occasional balanced feed for tall varieties
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead side shoots if growing for a single big bloom; none needed otherwise

GK-SUN-001.notes: Very fast from seed — a satisfying one for testers who want quick visible results.
GK-SUN-001.medium: Peat-free multipurpose compost; tall varieties benefit from a deep pot if grown in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow directly once frost risk has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water well and stake tall varieties against wind.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave seed heads for birds once flowering finishes.
seasonalActions.Winter: Nothing to do — it's an annual and will have finished its cycle.

problems.name: Slugs on seedlings
problems.fix: Protect young plants with barriers until they're established.

problems.name: Snapped stems in wind
problems.fix: Stake tall single-stem varieties early, before they're top-heavy.

propagation.season: Sow direct in late spring, or start indoors 3-4 weeks earlier for a head start.
propagation.methods: Large seeds are easy to handle and germinate quickly.
propagation.tips: Great first-sowing plant for new gardeners or testers.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: One of the best plants for bees and hoverflies — a single large head can host dozens of visits an hour at peak flowering.

timeline.Pruning: None — annual

gallery: Seed head with birds if captured

GK-SUN-001.weeklyFact: A sunflower head is actually made of thousands of tiny individual flowers arranged in a spiral pattern.
GK-SUN-001.pollinatorFact: Sunflowers are a magnet for bees and hoverflies, and the spent seed heads are a genuinely important food source for garden birds into autumn.
weeklyFacts: A sunflower head is actually made of thousands of tiny individual flowers arranged in a spiral pattern.
The tallest recorded sunflower grew to over 9 metres — roughly the height of a three-storey building.

GK-MAR-001.name: Marigold
GK-MAR-001.subtitle: Bright, reliable bedding annual, often used to companion-plant vegetables.

quickCare.Water: Water regularly, don't let it dry out completely
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed — too rich a feed reduces flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flowers to keep it blooming

GK-MAR-001.notes: Popular around vegetable beds for its reputed pest-deterrent scent, though evidence for this varies by pest.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow after frost risk has passed, or plant out young plants.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead regularly for continuous flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Pull once frosted and blackened.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual.

problems.name: Slugs on young plants
problems.fix: Protect seedlings with barriers until established.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Improve airflow and avoid overhead watering in humid weather.

propagation.season: Easy from seed sown indoors in spring or direct after frost.
propagation.methods: Save seed from spent flower heads for next year.
propagation.tips: Very forgiving for first-time propagators.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open-faced varieties are visited readily by hoverflies and bees; very double forms offer less access.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead through summer

GK-MAR-001.weeklyFact: Marigolds are one of the most common companion plants in vegetable gardens, often grown for their scent as much as their colour.
GK-MAR-001.pollinatorFact: Single and semi-double marigolds are useful for hoverflies and small bees; the very frilly double types are mostly for looks.
weeklyFacts: Marigolds are one of the most common companion plants in vegetable gardens, often grown for their scent as much as their colour.
'Marigold' is also used for pot marigold (Calendula), an entirely different plant — worth checking which one a recipe or companion-planting tip actually means.

GK-NAS-001.name: Nasturtium
GK-NAS-001.subtitle: Fast, edible, brilliantly easy — flowers, leaves and seed pods are all edible.

quickCare.Water: Water moderately — too much encourages leaves over flowers
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed — rich soil gives lots of leaf, few flowers
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; pinch trailing tips to bush out

GK-NAS-001.notes: Deliberately grow it in poor soil for the best flower display — this is one plant that punishes pampering.
GK-NAS-001.soil: Poor to average soil — avoid rich, fertile ground.
GK-NAS-001.medium: Peat-free compost is fine even without extra feeding.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow direct after frost risk has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water lightly and harvest leaves and flowers for salads.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Collect ripe seed pods to resow next year.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished by first frost.

problems.name: Blackfly
problems.fix: Very attractive to blackfly, which is sometimes used deliberately as a 'trap crop' to protect other plants nearby.

problems.name: Cabbage white caterpillars
problems.fix: Nasturtium is a preferred host — leaves get shredded, which again can protect other brassicas nearby.

propagation.season: Large seeds sow directly with no fuss — one of the easiest plants to grow from seed.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds readily; you may get volunteers next year.
propagation.tips: Great for children or absolute beginner testers.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Simple, open flowers are easily accessed by bees, and the plant is also a valuable 'sacrificial' host for blackfly and caterpillars.

timeline.Pruning: None — annual

gallery: Trailing habit in a pot or border

GK-NAS-001.weeklyFact: Every part of a nasturtium above ground is edible — the peppery leaves and flowers are a genuine salad ingredient, not just a garnish.
GK-NAS-001.pollinatorFact: Nasturtium flowers are simple and open, making nectar easy to reach for bees, and the plant is often used as a deliberate decoy to draw blackfly away from other crops.
weeklyFacts: Every part of a nasturtium above ground is edible — the peppery leaves and flowers are a genuine salad ingredient, not just a garnish.
Nasturtium seeds were historically pickled and used as a substitute for capers.

GK-CLE-001.name: Clematis
GK-CLE-001.subtitle: Climbing perennial for walls, fences and obelisks.

quickCare.Water: Water deeply and regularly, especially in the first two years
quickCare.Light: Flowers in sun, but roots and base like to stay cool and shaded
quickCare.Feeding: Spring feed with a balanced or potash-rich fertiliser
quickCare.Pruning: Pruning group depends on variety — check before cutting back hard

GK-CLE-001.notes: Clematis famously like 'head in the sun, feet in the shade' — shade the root area with other planting or a flat stone.
GK-CLE-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for container growing; needs a large, deep pot.

seasonalActions.Spring: Growth accelerates fast — tie in new shoots regularly.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water deeply in dry spells, especially newly planted specimens.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Tidy dead growth once flowering finishes, depending on pruning group.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune group 3 varieties hard in late winter, if applicable.

problems.name: Clematis wilt
problems.fix: Sudden collapse of stems — cut back to healthy growth below the damage, most recover the following season.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Improve airflow and avoid letting the compost dry out and rewet erratically.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer using a leaf-bud method.
propagation.methods: Layering a low stem into the soil is a reliable, low-effort method.
propagation.tips: Confirm the pruning group before buying — it changes how and when you cut back.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Many varieties are good for bees, particularly single-flowered types with visible stamens.

timeline.Pruning: Depends on pruning group — check before cutting

gallery: Seed heads if a variety that produces them

GK-CLE-001.weeklyFact: Clematis seed heads can be as decorative as the flowers — some varieties are grown almost as much for their fluffy autumn seed heads.
GK-CLE-001.pollinatorFact: Single, open-flowered clematis varieties give bees good access to pollen; very double forms are more for show.
weeklyFacts: Clematis seed heads can be as decorative as the flowers — some varieties are grown almost as much for their fluffy autumn seed heads.
There are over 300 species of clematis, ranging from tiny alpine types to vigorous climbers that can cover a whole wall.

GK-CAM-001.name: Camellia
GK-CAM-001.subtitle: Glossy evergreen shrub with striking late-winter to spring flowers.

quickCare.Water: Water well, especially in pots — never let it dry out during bud formation in late summer
quickCare.Light: Bright, part shade; avoid early morning sun after frost, which can damage buds
quickCare.Feeding: Ericaceous feed in spring and after flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Prune lightly after flowering to shape; avoid cutting into old wood

GK-CAM-001.notes: Needs acidic soil — grow in a pot with ericaceous compost if your garden soil is alkaline.
GK-CAM-001.medium: Ericaceous peat-free compost, essential for pot-grown camellias.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowering finishes; feed and deadhead.
seasonalActions.Summer: Buds form for next year — keep watering consistent, this is the critical stage.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Continue steady watering as next year's buds develop.
seasonalActions.Winter: Flowers may open depending on variety; protect from hard frost and morning sun on buds.

problems.name: Bud drop
problems.fix: Usually from irregular watering or a sudden cold snap; keep watering consistent through summer bud formation.

problems.name: Yellowing leaves (chlorosis)
problems.fix: Sign of alkaline soil or water; feed with ericaceous fertiliser and use rainwater if possible.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer, though camellias can be slow and tricky to root at home.
propagation.methods: Layering a low branch is a more reliable amateur method.
propagation.tips: Patience is genuinely required — camellias are not a fast propagation project.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Many camellia flowers, especially very double forms, offer little accessible pollen; single varieties are somewhat better.

timeline.Flowering: Late winter to spring depending on variety
timeline.Pruning: Lightly, just after flowering

GK-CAM-001.weeklyFact: Camellia flowers can survive a light frost, but a sudden hard freeze after a mild spell can turn open blooms brown overnight.
GK-CAM-001.pollinatorFact: Double camellia flowers are showy but often nearly sterile for pollinators; single-flowered varieties give better access to what pollen is available.
weeklyFacts: Camellia flowers can survive a light frost, but a sudden hard freeze after a mild spell can turn open blooms brown overnight.
Camellia sinensis, a close relative of the ornamental camellia, is the plant that gives us all true tea — black, green and white.

GK-RHO-001.name: Rhododendron
GK-RHO-001.subtitle: Bold spring shrub for acidic, woodland-style planting.

quickCare.Water: Water well, especially newly planted specimens and those in pots
quickCare.Light: Dappled or part shade; avoid deep shade (poor flowering) or hot midday sun (scorch)
quickCare.Feeding: Ericaceous feed in spring
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent trusses; avoid heavy pruning except to renovate old plants

GK-RHO-001.notes: Shallow-rooted — mulch generously rather than digging near the base, and never let it dry out in the first two summers.
GK-RHO-001.soil: Acidic, humus-rich, moisture-retentive but free-draining soil.

seasonalActions.Spring: Deadhead spent flower trusses carefully to avoid damaging next year's buds just behind them.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water deeply in dry spells, especially shallow-rooted young plants.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Mulch with ericaceous compost or leaf mould.
seasonalActions.Winter: Buds are largely set and dormant; protect from harsh drying wind.

problems.name: Bud blast
problems.fix: Buds turn brown and fail to open, sometimes linked to leafhopper damage; remove affected buds.

problems.name: Vine weevil
problems.fix: Notched leaf edges (adults) or collapsed plants in pots (grubs eating roots); check compost and consider nematode treatment.

propagation.season: Layering a low branch into acidic compost is the most reliable amateur method.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer are possible but can be slow to root.
propagation.tips: Species types can be grown from seed, but named hybrids won't come true.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Tubular, nectar-rich flowers attract bees, particularly bumblebees able to access the deeper flower shape.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering only

gallery: New growth flush in late spring

GK-RHO-001.weeklyFact: Some rhododendron species contain toxic compounds in their nectar, which is where the old term 'mad honey' comes from historically.
GK-RHO-001.pollinatorFact: Bumblebees are particularly well suited to rhododendron's tubular flower shape and are among its most frequent visitors.
weeklyFacts: Some rhododendron species contain toxic compounds in their nectar, which is where the old term 'mad honey' comes from historically.
The word rhododendron comes from Greek for 'rose tree,' even though it's not related to roses at all.

GK-CRO-001.name: Crocosmia
GK-CRO-001.subtitle: Fiery, arching summer flowers from a spreading corm.

quickCare.Water: Water in dry spells; otherwise fairly drought-tolerant once established
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed; too rich and it flops
quickCare.Pruning: Cut old foliage down in late autumn once it has died back

GK-CRO-001.notes: Spreads readily once established — divide every few years if it starts to congest.
GK-CRO-001.medium: Peat-free compost is fine; not a fussy plant once settled.

seasonalActions.Spring: New shoots emerge fast — divide congested clumps now if needed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in prolonged dry spells; deadhead if tidiness matters to you.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back foliage once it has died down after flowering.
seasonalActions.Winter: Corms are dormant; a mulch helps in colder gardens.

problems.name: Spider mite in hot, dry summers
problems.fix: Increase humidity around the plant and hose foliage down in very dry spells.

problems.name: Frost damage in cold gardens
problems.fix: Mulch the base in autumn in areas prone to hard frost.

propagation.season: Divide congested corm clumps in spring.
propagation.methods: Each division should include a few healthy corms.
propagation.tips: Very forgiving — hard to get this one wrong.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Arching, tubular flowers are popular with bees, and the seed heads are useful for birds in autumn.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back in late autumn

GK-CRO-001.weeklyFact: Crocosmia was originally bred from South African species and has since naturalised so successfully in parts of the UK and Ireland that some varieties are considered near-wild.
GK-CRO-001.pollinatorFact: Crocosmia's tubular orange-red flowers are a strong draw for bees able to access the shape, and the seed heads that follow feed birds into autumn.
weeklyFacts: Crocosmia was originally bred from South African species and has since naturalised so successfully in parts of the UK and Ireland that some varieties are considered near-wild.
Crocosmia corms form in a chain, each year's corm growing on top of the last — lifting one often reveals several seasons' history.

GK-NEP-001.name: Nepeta
GK-NEP-001.subtitle: Soft, silvery, long-flowering border edging that pollinators love.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water only in prolonged dry spells
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding — too rich a soil causes floppy growth
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back hard after first flush to encourage a strong second flowering

GK-NEP-001.notes: Also genuinely attracts cats, who may roll on it enthusiastically — worth knowing before planting near a vegetable bed.
GK-NEP-001.soil: Free-draining soil; tolerates poor, dry conditions well.
GK-NEP-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit if growing in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut old growth back hard to fresh shoots at the base.
seasonalActions.Summer: Trim after the first flush of flowers for a strong repeat bloom.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave some growth in place for winter structure and wildlife.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave standing over winter; cut back in spring instead.

problems.name: Floppy, sprawling growth
problems.fix: Usually too rich a soil or too much shade; move to leaner, sunnier conditions.

problems.name: Powdery mildew in humid summers
problems.fix: Improve airflow and avoid overhead watering.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in late spring, or division of established clumps in spring or autumn.
propagation.methods: Very easy to root from cuttings on a windowsill.
propagation.tips: One of the most forgiving perennials to propagate at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An outstanding bee plant — long flowering season and easily accessible tubular flowers make it one of the best border plants for pollinators.

timeline.Pruning: Hard cut-back in spring, trim after first flush

gallery: Bees or pollinators visiting if captured

GK-NEP-001.weeklyFact: Nepeta is in the same plant family as true mint, which is part of why bees find it just as irresistible.
GK-NEP-001.pollinatorFact: Nepeta is consistently rated among the best garden plants for bees — a long flowering season combined with easily accessible nectar makes it a reliable pollinator magnet.
weeklyFacts: Nepeta is in the same plant family as true mint, which is part of why bees find it just as irresistible.
The compound that makes cats roll around in catmint, nepetalactone, is being studied by scientists as a possible natural insect repellent.

GK-AST-001.name: Astilbe
GK-AST-001.subtitle: Feathery flower plumes for damp, shaded borders.

quickCare.Water: Keep consistently moist — this plant does not tolerate drying out, even briefly
quickCare.Light: Part to full shade
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed in spring
quickCare.Pruning: Cut flower plumes down once faded, or leave for winter structure

GK-AST-001.notes: The single biggest astilbe mistake is letting it dry out — it will scorch and collapse quickly in dry soil.
GK-AST-001.soil: Moist, humus-rich soil that never fully dries out.
GK-AST-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould or organic matter for moisture retention.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth emerges — keep consistently watered from the start.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water is critical; check daily in containers during warm spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back once flower plumes fade, or leave standing for texture.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant; fully hardy and needs no protection.

problems.name: Leaf scorch
problems.fix: Brown, crispy leaf edges mean the soil has dried out — water more consistently and mulch to retain moisture.

problems.name: Slugs on new spring growth
problems.fix: Protect emerging shoots with barriers or torchlight removal.

propagation.season: Divide established clumps in spring or autumn.
propagation.methods: Each division needs a healthy piece of crown with roots attached.
propagation.tips: Water divisions in well and keep consistently moist until re-established.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Flowers offer some nectar for smaller pollinators, though the tight plume structure limits access compared to more open flower shapes.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back after flowering or leave for winter interest

gallery: Whole clump in a shaded border
Winter seed heads if left standing

GK-AST-001.weeklyFact: Astilbe plumes are made up of hundreds of tiny individual flowers, which is why they read as feathery from a distance but detailed up close.
GK-AST-001.pollinatorFact: Astilbe's dense flower plumes offer modest nectar for smaller pollinators, though the tightly packed structure makes access harder than more open flower shapes.
weeklyFacts: Astilbe plumes are made up of hundreds of tiny individual flowers, which is why they read as feathery from a distance but detailed up close.
Astilbe plumes hold their shape well when dried, making them a popular choice for dried flower arrangements.

GK-PLI-001.name: Peace Lily
GK-PLI-001.subtitle: Elegant, shade-tolerant houseplant that tells you clearly when it's thirsty.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top of the compost feels dry; the leaves droop dramatically when thirsty and recover fast once watered
quickCare.Light: Bright, indirect light; tolerates low light but flowers less
quickCare.Feeding: Light monthly feed spring to summer
quickCare.Pruning: Remove yellowing leaves and spent flower spikes at the base

GK-PLI-001.notes: One of the most forgiving houseplants — the dramatic drooping when thirsty is actually a useful, hard-to-miss watering signal.
GK-PLI-001.soil: N/A — grown in potting compost, not garden soil.
GK-PLI-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with good drainage; avoid waterlogging.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume regular feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often in warmer, drier indoor conditions.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water less; let the compost dry out further between waterings.

problems.name: Drooping leaves
problems.fix: Almost always underwatering — water thoroughly and it typically recovers within hours.

problems.name: Brown leaf tips
problems.fix: Usually low humidity or a build-up of minerals from tap water; mist occasionally or use rainwater.

propagation.season: Divide the whole plant at repotting time, splitting the root ball into sections with leaves attached.
propagation.methods: Each division should have a healthy cluster of roots and leaves.
propagation.tips: Spring is the best time to divide, alongside repotting.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental and air quality.

timeline.Flowering: Can flower year-round indoors, often more in brighter light
timeline.Pruning: Remove spent flower spikes and yellow leaves as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Peace Lily.

GK-PLI-001.weeklyFact: A drooping peace lily looks alarming but is one of the clearest 'water me now' signals in the houseplant world — it usually stands back up within a few hours of watering.
GK-PLI-001.pollinatorFact: Peace lilies are grown indoors and have no access to outdoor pollinators; their value is purely ornamental and for indoor air quality.
weeklyFacts: A drooping peace lily looks alarming but is one of the clearest 'water me now' signals in the houseplant world — it usually stands back up within a few hours of watering.
Despite the name, peace lilies aren't true lilies at all — they're in the arum family, related to calla lilies and philodendrons.

GK-SNK-001.name: Snake Plant
GK-SNK-001.subtitle: Architectural, near-indestructible houseplant for low-light corners.

quickCare.Water: Water sparingly — allow compost to dry out fully between waterings; overwatering is the main way to kill this plant
quickCare.Light: Tolerates low light well; grows faster in bright indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in summer only, and only if you want faster growth
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; remove any damaged leaves at the base

GK-SNK-001.notes: The single most common way to kill a snake plant is overwatering — when in doubt, wait longer.
GK-SNK-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent-style compost; avoid moisture-retentive mixes.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding if faster growth is wanted.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water slightly more often in warm conditions, still allowing full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering further as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less is often enough.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: By far the most common issue, caused by overwatering or poor drainage; repot into a free-draining mix and water much less.

problems.name: Soft, mushy leaf base
problems.fix: Sign of rot from overwatering — remove affected leaves and let the compost dry out fully.

propagation.season: Divide the rhizome at the base when repotting, ensuring each division has roots attached.
propagation.methods: Leaf cuttings will also root, though variegated types often revert to plain green.
propagation.tips: Very slow-growing, so propagation takes patience.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental and air quality.

timeline.Pruning: Remove damaged leaves as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Snake Plant.

GK-SNK-001.weeklyFact: Snake plants are sometimes called 'mother-in-law's tongue' for their sharp, pointed leaves — and they're famously hard to kill through neglect.
GK-SNK-001.pollinatorFact: Snake plants are grown indoors and have no access to outdoor pollinators; their value is purely ornamental and for indoor air quality.
weeklyFacts: Snake plants are sometimes called 'mother-in-law's tongue' for their sharp, pointed leaves — and they're famously hard to kill through neglect.
Botanists reclassified snake plants from the genus Sansevieria into Dracaena in recent years, though most gardeners still know it by the old name.

GK-MON-001.name: Monstera
GK-MON-001.subtitle: Bold, iconic split-leaf houseplant that gets genuinely large.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top few centimetres of compost are dry; likes consistent moisture but hates sitting wet
quickCare.Light: Bright, indirect light; leaf splitting improves with more light
quickCare.Feeding: Monthly feed spring to summer
quickCare.Pruning: Remove yellowing or damaged leaves; provide a moss pole for climbing support

GK-MON-001.notes: Leaf splitting (fenestration) increases with better light and plant maturity — a plant kept in low light stays solid-leaved much longer.
GK-MON-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with added bark or perlite for airy, well-draining roots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume regular feeding as growth accelerates.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often; this is peak growing season.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; let compost dry out more between waterings.

problems.name: Yellowing lower leaves
problems.fix: Often overwatering — check drainage and reduce watering frequency.

problems.name: Brown crispy leaf edges
problems.fix: Usually low humidity or a draught; mist occasionally or move away from radiators.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings with at least one node and aerial root will root readily in water or moist compost.
propagation.methods: Air layering on a mature stem is an option for larger specimens.
propagation.tips: New leaves take several weeks to unfurl fully after a cutting roots.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Pruning: Prune to control size and remove damaged leaves

GK-MON-001.weeklyFact: In the wild, Monstera deliciosa is a climbing vine that can reach many metres up a tree trunk, using aerial roots the whole way.
GK-MON-001.pollinatorFact: Monstera is grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its value is purely ornamental as a foliage statement plant.
weeklyFacts: In the wild, Monstera deliciosa is a climbing vine that can reach many metres up a tree trunk, using aerial roots the whole way.
The species name 'deliciosa' refers to the plant's edible fruit, which is a genuine (if rarely grown) tropical treat when fully ripe.

GK-POH-001.name: Pothos
GK-POH-001.subtitle: Trailing, near-unkillable houseplant, great for beginners and shelves.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top of the compost is dry; tolerates occasional missed waterings well
quickCare.Light: Tolerates low light but variegation is stronger in bright indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light monthly feed in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Trim trailing stems to control length; pinch tips to encourage bushiness

GK-POH-001.notes: One of the most forgiving houseplants going — a good first plant for a tester who says they 'kill everything'.
GK-POH-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with reasonable drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water slightly more often; trim trailing stems as needed.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; growth slows noticeably indoors over winter.

problems.name: Leggy growth with few leaves
problems.fix: Usually too little light; move closer to a bright window and trim to encourage bushier regrowth.

problems.name: Yellow leaves
problems.fix: Most often overwatering; let the compost dry out more between waterings.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings with a node root very easily in a glass of water within a few weeks.
propagation.methods: Once rooted, pot up several cuttings together for a fuller-looking plant.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest houseplants to propagate and share.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Pruning: Trim trailing stems as needed to control shape

GK-POH-001.weeklyFact: Pothos is sometimes nicknamed 'devil's ivy' because it stays green even in low light and is famously difficult to kill.
GK-POH-001.pollinatorFact: Pothos is grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its value is purely ornamental and it's a popular beginner plant.
weeklyFacts: Pothos is sometimes nicknamed 'devil's ivy' because it stays green even in low light and is famously difficult to kill.
Pothos rarely flowers indoors because it needs the kind of intense tropical light and maturity it would only reach climbing a rainforest tree.

GK-SPI-001.name: Spider Plant
GK-SPI-001.subtitle: Fast-growing, forgiving houseplant that produces its own baby plantlets.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top of the compost is dry; sensitive to fluoride/chlorine in tap water, which can cause brown tips
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light; tolerates lower light but grows slower
quickCare.Feeding: Light monthly feed spring to summer
quickCare.Pruning: Trim any brown leaf tips; remove plantlets once they've rooted if not wanted

GK-SPI-001.notes: If leaf tips keep browning, try switching to rainwater or filtered water — often a mineral sensitivity, not a care mistake.
GK-SPI-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with reasonable drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume feeding; watch for new plantlets forming on flower stems.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often; plantlets can be potted up once they show roots.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; growth slows indoors over winter.

problems.name: Brown leaf tips
problems.fix: Usually tap water minerals or fluoride; switch to rainwater or let tap water stand overnight before using.

problems.name: Pale, washed-out leaves
problems.fix: Too much direct sun; move to bright but indirect light.

propagation.season: Plantlets that form on long flower stems can be potted directly, or rooted in water first.
propagation.methods: Extremely easy and fast — often rooting within 1-2 weeks.
propagation.tips: A great propagation project for testers, since success is almost guaranteed.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Flowering: Small white flowers on trailing stems, followed by plantlets
timeline.Pruning: Trim brown tips as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Spider Plant.

GK-SPI-001.weeklyFact: Those dangling 'baby' plantlets are genetically identical clones of the parent plant, each capable of rooting into a full new spider plant.
GK-SPI-001.pollinatorFact: Spider plants are grown indoors and have no access to outdoor pollinators; their small flowers are rarely seen and mostly a curiosity indoors.
weeklyFacts: Those dangling 'baby' plantlets are genetically identical clones of the parent plant, each capable of rooting into a full new spider plant.
Spider plants were one of the species tested in NASA's 1989 Clean Air Study for their ability to filter indoor air pollutants.

GK-ALO-001.name: Aloe Vera
GK-ALO-001.subtitle: Sun-loving succulent with genuinely useful sap, if you can avoid overwatering it.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out completely — this is the single most common care mistake with aloe
quickCare.Light: Bright light, ideally some direct sun by a window
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in summer only
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any damaged or collapsed outer leaves at the base

GK-ALO-001.notes: Overwatering is by far the most common cause of aloe problems — when unsure, wait longer before the next watering.
GK-ALO-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost, ideally with extra grit or perlite.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding if wanted.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but still allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less is often plenty.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: Almost always from overwatering or poor drainage; repot into a free-draining mix and reduce watering significantly.

problems.name: Thin, stretched leaves (etiolation)
problems.fix: Sign of too little light; move to a brighter spot, ideally with some direct sun.

propagation.season: Remove and pot up offset 'pups' that form at the base once they have their own small roots.
propagation.methods: Let any cut surface callus over for a day before potting.
propagation.tips: Offsets are the easiest and most reliable propagation method.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators; occasionally flowers outdoors in warm climates, attracting sunbirds and bees where hardy.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional tall flower spike if grown in very bright conditions
timeline.Pruning: Remove damaged outer leaves as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Aloe Vera.

GK-ALO-001.weeklyFact: The clear gel inside an aloe leaf has been used for skin care for thousands of years — just be sure to avoid the yellow sap layer just under the skin, which can irritate.
GK-ALO-001.pollinatorFact: Indoors, aloe rarely flowers and has no pollinator access; in warm outdoor climates its tall flower spikes attract bees and, in some regions, sunbirds.
weeklyFacts: The clear gel inside an aloe leaf has been used for skin care for thousands of years — just be sure to avoid the yellow sap layer just under the skin, which can irritate.
There are over 500 species of aloe, but Aloe vera is the one most commonly grown for its skin-soothing gel.

GK-ZZP-001.name: ZZ Plant
GK-ZZP-001.subtitle: Glossy, extremely low-maintenance houseplant for low light and forgetful waterers.

quickCare.Water: Water sparingly, letting compost dry out fully between waterings — grows from a water-storing rhizome so tolerates neglect well
quickCare.Light: Tolerates low light; grows faster with brighter indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in summer only
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; remove any yellowing stems at the base

GK-ZZP-001.notes: Genuinely one of the most drought-tolerant common houseplants — better to underwater than overwater this one.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding if faster growth is wanted.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water slightly more often, still allowing full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering further.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Yellowing stems
problems.fix: Almost always overwatering; the plant stores water in its rhizome and doesn't need frequent watering.

problems.name: Slow growth
problems.fix: Normal — ZZ plants are naturally slow-growing, especially in lower light; this isn't usually a problem to fix.

propagation.season: Individual leaflets can be rooted in water or compost, though it's slow — often months before a new rhizome forms.
propagation.methods: Dividing an established rhizome clump at repotting is faster and more reliable.
propagation.tips: Patience is essential with this one.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Pruning: Remove any yellowing stems at the base

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of ZZ Plant.

GK-ZZP-001.weeklyFact: ZZ plants grow from a thick underground rhizome that stores water, which is exactly why they shrug off missed waterings so well.
GK-ZZP-001.pollinatorFact: ZZ plants are grown indoors and have no access to outdoor pollinators; their value is purely ornamental.
weeklyFacts: ZZ plants grow from a thick underground rhizome that stores water, which is exactly why they shrug off missed waterings so well.
ZZ plant's full botanical name, Zamioculcas zamiifolia, describes its leaves as resembling those of a cycad (Zamia).

GK-ORC-001.name: Orchid
GK-ORC-001.subtitle: Elegant flowering houseplant, grown in bark rather than soil.

quickCare.Water: Water by soaking roots for a few minutes weekly, then drain fully — never leave sitting in water
quickCare.Light: Bright, indirect light; avoid direct midday sun which scorches leaves
quickCare.Feeding: Dilute orchid-specific feed every other watering during growth
quickCare.Pruning: Cut the flower spike back to a node after flowering to encourage reblooming, or to the base if it yellows

GK-ORC-001.notes: Grown in bark, not compost — this is the single biggest thing testers new to orchids get wrong by potting into ordinary soil.
GK-ORC-001.soil: N/A — epiphytic, not grown in soil.
GK-ORC-001.medium: Specialist orchid bark mix; never use ordinary potting compost.

seasonalActions.Spring: Watch for a new flower spike emerging from between the leaves.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water and feed regularly during active growth.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water less often; many orchids flower in winter, so watch for a spike.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: From sitting in water or overly frequent watering; healthy roots should be silvery-green, not brown and mushy.

problems.name: No flowers returning
problems.fix: Often insufficient light or a spike cut at the wrong point; ensure bright indirect light and leave a node when cutting back.

propagation.season: Backbud (keiki) plantlets sometimes form on the flower spike and can be potted once they have roots.
propagation.methods: Division of very large multi-crown specimens is possible but not common at home.
propagation.tips: Most home growers simply keep the original plant going for years rather than propagating.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors, so not accessible to outdoor pollinators; in the wild these orchids are pollinated by specific moths, hence the common name.

timeline.Flowering: Can flower for months at a time, often winter to spring
timeline.Pruning: Cut spike to a node after flowering to encourage reblooming

gallery: Root system through a clear pot

GK-ORC-001.weeklyFact: 'Moth orchid' comes from the flower's resemblance to a moth in flight — and in the wild, these orchids are pollinated by night-flying moths.
GK-ORC-001.pollinatorFact: Indoors, orchids have no pollinator access; in their native habitat, moth orchids rely on specific night-flying moths for pollination.
weeklyFacts: 'Moth orchid' comes from the flower's resemblance to a moth in flight — and in the wild, these orchids are pollinated by night-flying moths.
The orchid family is one of the largest of all flowering plant families, with over 25,000 known species worldwide.

GK-ECV-001.name: Echeveria
GK-ECV-001.subtitle: Classic rosette-shaped succulent, ideal for sunny windowsills and mixed planters.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out completely; water at the base to avoid rot in the rosette centre
quickCare.Light: Bright light, ideally some direct sun for compact, colourful growth
quickCare.Feeding: Very light feed once in summer, if at all
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any dead lower leaves; snip and repot 'stretched' rosettes to restart

GK-ECV-001.notes: Water at the base, not over the top — water sitting in the rosette centre is a common cause of rot.
GK-ECV-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light watering as light levels increase.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always let compost dry out fully between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as light drops.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Stretched, leggy rosette (etiolation)
problems.fix: Sign of too little light; move to a brighter spot — the plant can be cut and restarted to fix the shape.

problems.name: Mushy leaves
problems.fix: Overwatering or water sitting in the rosette centre; reduce watering and always water at the base.

propagation.season: Individual leaves can be gently twisted off, dried for a day, and laid on gritty compost to root.
propagation.methods: Offsets that form around the base can be removed and potted separately.
propagation.tips: A satisfying, low-risk propagation project — most healthy leaves will root.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown mainly as an ornamental foliage succulent; outdoor flowering stems can attract some pollinators in warm climates.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional tall flower spike with small bell-shaped flowers
timeline.Pruning: Remove dead lower leaves as needed

GK-ECV-001.weeklyFact: Echeveria rosettes are a textbook example of a Fibonacci spiral in nature — the leaves are arranged in a precise mathematical pattern.
