GK-ECV-001.pollinatorFact: When echeveria does flower, the small bell-shaped blooms on tall stems can attract bees and hummingbirds in warm outdoor climates; indoors it's grown mainly for its rosette shape.
weeklyFacts: Echeveria rosettes are a textbook example of a Fibonacci spiral in nature — the leaves are arranged in a precise mathematical pattern.
Echeveria is named after Atanasio Echeverría y Godoy, an 18th-century Mexican botanical illustrator.

GK-SED-001.name: Sedum
GK-SED-001.subtitle: Tough, trailing or upright succulent, useful for pots, borders and green roofs.

quickCare.Water: Very drought-tolerant once established; water sparingly, especially outdoors where rainfall is often enough
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the most compact, colourful growth
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed
quickCare.Pruning: Trim to shape; remove dead growth in spring

GK-SED-001.notes: One of the toughest, most forgiving plants in this library — a good choice for a tester who wants low-effort success.
GK-SED-001.soil: Free-draining soil or compost; tolerates poor soil well.
GK-SED-001.medium: Cactus/succulent compost with added grit if potted.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth resumes; trim to shape if needed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Very drought-tolerant; water only in prolonged dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Some varieties flower into autumn — deadhead if wanted.
seasonalActions.Winter: Many outdoor sedums are fully hardy and need no protection.

problems.name: Root rot in poorly drained soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage or move to a gritty mix; this is the main way to lose a sedum.

problems.name: Aphids on flower buds
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root very easily — just let the cut end callus for a day and place on gritty compost.
propagation.methods: Division of established clumps works well for spreading types.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest plants in the library to propagate.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Outdoor flowering sedums, especially larger border types, are excellent late-season nectar sources for bees and butterflies.

timeline.Flowering: Late summer to autumn for many outdoor varieties
timeline.Pruning: Trim to shape in spring

GK-SED-001.weeklyFact: Some larger border sedums are among the last strong nectar sources of the year, often still buzzing with bees and butterflies into October.
GK-SED-001.pollinatorFact: Larger outdoor sedum varieties are genuinely valuable late-season plants for bees and butterflies, flowering when many other nectar sources have finished.
weeklyFacts: Some larger border sedums are among the last strong nectar sources of the year, often still buzzing with bees and butterflies into October.
Sedum is sometimes called 'stonecrop' because many species naturally grow in rocky, gritty ground where little else survives.

GK-HAW-001.name: Haworthia
GK-HAW-001.subtitle: Small, patterned succulent that tolerates lower light better than most.
tags: Low light tolerant for a succulent

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully; slightly more forgiving of lower light than most succulents, but still needs a well-drained mix
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light; avoid harsh direct sun which can scorch leaves
quickCare.Feeding: Very light feed once in summer, if at all
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any dead outer leaves at the base

GK-HAW-001.notes: A good succulent choice for a bright windowsill that doesn't get direct sun — more shade-tolerant than most succulents.
GK-HAW-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light watering as light levels increase.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always let compost dry out between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as light drops.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Sunburn (brown patches)
problems.fix: From sudden direct sun exposure; move to bright but indirect light.

problems.name: Mushy base
problems.fix: Overwatering; reduce frequency and ensure the pot has good drainage.

propagation.season: Offsets ('pups') form readily around the base and can be gently separated and potted once rooted.
propagation.methods: Let any cut surface callus for a day before potting.
propagation.tips: One of the easier succulents to propagate reliably.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown mainly as an ornamental foliage succulent indoors, with limited pollinator relevance.

timeline.Pruning: Remove dead outer leaves as needed

GK-HAW-001.weeklyFact: Haworthia's translucent leaf tips are sometimes called 'windows' — in the wild they let light into the leaf while most of the plant stays buried in sand.
GK-HAW-001.pollinatorFact: Haworthia is grown mainly indoors for its patterned foliage; its small flower spikes have limited pollinator relevance in a typical indoor setting.
weeklyFacts: Haworthia's translucent leaf tips are sometimes called 'windows' — in the wild they let light into the leaf while most of the plant stays buried in sand.
Haworthia is named after Adrian Hardy Haworth, an English entomologist and botanist who studied succulents extensively.

GK-JAD-001.name: Jade Plant
GK-JAD-001.subtitle: Chunky, tree-like succulent that can live for decades with minimal fuss.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully between waterings
quickCare.Light: Bright light, ideally some direct sun for compact, sturdy growth
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in summer
quickCare.Pruning: Prune to shape — jade plants respond well to shaping like a small tree

GK-JAD-001.notes: Genuinely long-lived — with steady care a jade plant can outlast the rest of the trial plants in this library by decades.
GK-JAD-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit; a heavier pot helps balance top growth.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding and watering as growth resumes.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Leaf drop
problems.fix: Usually a sudden change in light, temperature or watering; keep conditions as stable as possible.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: From overwatering or poor drainage; repot into a grittier mix and water less often.

propagation.season: Individual leaves or stem cuttings root easily after the cut end has callused for a day or two.
propagation.methods: Larger stem cuttings establish faster than single leaves.
propagation.tips: A popular gift plant to propagate and share, since cuttings root so reliably.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Occasionally flowers indoors or outdoors in mild climates with small star-shaped blooms that attract bees where accessible.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional winter flowering in mature, well-lit specimens
timeline.Pruning: Prune to shape as needed, any time of year

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Jade Plant.

GK-JAD-001.weeklyFact: Jade plants are sometimes called 'money plants' and are commonly given as gifts believed to bring good luck and prosperity.

weeklyFacts: Jade plants are sometimes called 'money plants' and are commonly given as gifts believed to bring good luck and prosperity.
In some feng shui traditions, a jade plant is placed near the entrance of a home or business for good fortune.

GK-AEO-001.name: Aeonium
GK-AEO-001.subtitle: Striking architectural succulent with rosettes held on visible stems.

quickCare.Water: Water moderately in winter growth period; reduce sharply in its summer semi-dormancy
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best colour and compact rosettes
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed during active winter growth only
quickCare.Pruning: Remove dead lower leaves; trim leggy stems to encourage branching

GK-AEO-001.notes: Unusual among succulents — aeonium actively grows in cooler months and rests in high summer heat, the opposite of many other succulents.
GK-AEO-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Growth is slowing as temperatures rise — ease off watering.
seasonalActions.Summer: Semi-dormant in hot weather; water sparingly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Growth resumes as temperatures cool — increase watering gradually.
seasonalActions.Winter: Main active growing season; water moderately and feed lightly.

problems.name: Leaf drop in high summer heat
problems.fix: Often normal semi-dormancy; reduce watering rather than increasing it.

problems.name: Leggy stems reaching for light
problems.fix: Move to a brighter spot; leggy stems can be cut and re-rooted to restart the rosette.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root well — let the cut end callus for a couple of days before placing on gritty compost.
propagation.methods: Offsets at the base of some varieties can be removed and potted.
propagation.tips: Best propagated during its active winter growth period.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown mainly as an ornamental architectural succulent, with limited typical indoor pollinator relevance.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional yellow flower cluster from mature rosettes
timeline.Pruning: Trim leggy stems to encourage branching, any time of year

GK-AEO-001.weeklyFact: Aeonium runs on an unusual seasonal clock compared to most succulents — actively growing in cool winter months and resting through summer heat.
GK-AEO-001.pollinatorFact: Aeonium's occasional yellow flower clusters can attract bees where grown outdoors in mild climates; indoors it's grown mainly for its architectural rosette form.
weeklyFacts: Aeonium runs on an unusual seasonal clock compared to most succulents — actively growing in cool winter months and resting through summer heat.
Aeonium rosettes close up tightly in summer heat and open flat again once cooler weather returns.

GK-TOM-001.name: Tomato
GK-TOM-001.subtitle: Classic summer crop, best grown up a support with regular feeding.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently — irregular watering causes split fruit and blossom end rot
quickCare.Light: Full sun, as much as possible
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly high-potash tomato feed once first fruit trusses form
quickCare.Pruning: Remove side shoots on cordon varieties weekly; remove lower leaves as the plant grows

GK-TOM-001.notes: Consistency is everything with tomatoes — irregular watering is the single biggest cause of split or poor-quality fruit.
GK-TOM-001.medium: Peat-free compost enriched with garden compost or manure-based improver for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in warmth; pot on as seedlings grow.
seasonalActions.Summer: Plant out after frost, tie in to supports, remove side shoots weekly and feed.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Harvest remaining fruit; ripen green tomatoes indoors before first frost.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Blossom end rot
problems.fix: Dark, sunken patch on the fruit base from inconsistent watering, not usually a calcium deficiency in the soil itself — water more consistently.

problems.name: Blight
problems.fix: Dark blotches on leaves and stems in wet, humid weather; remove affected foliage promptly and avoid overhead watering.

propagation.season: Sow seed indoors in early spring on a warm windowsill or heated propagator.
propagation.methods: Side shoots removed from cordon varieties will root in water and grow into new plants.
propagation.tips: Harden off young plants gradually before planting outside.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Tomato flowers are 'buzz pollinated' — bumblebees vibrate the flower to release pollen, which is far more effective than wind alone.

timeline.Pruning: Remove side shoots weekly through summer

GK-TOM-001.weeklyFact: Tomato flowers need to be vibrated to release their pollen — bumblebees do this naturally, which is why greenhouse growers sometimes use a small vibrating 'wand' instead.
GK-TOM-001.pollinatorFact: Tomato flowers rely on 'buzz pollination' from bumblebees, which vibrate the flower at a specific frequency to release pollen that simply falls out otherwise.
weeklyFacts: Tomato flowers need to be vibrated to release their pollen — bumblebees do this naturally, which is why greenhouse growers sometimes use a small vibrating 'wand' instead.
Tomatoes were once widely believed to be poisonous in parts of 18th-century Europe, since they're in the same family as deadly nightshade.

GK-ONI-001.name: Onion
GK-ONI-001.subtitle: Reliable, low-fuss crop grown from small bulb sets rather than seed.

quickCare.Water: Water in dry spells while bulbs are swelling; stop watering as foliage yellows to help bulbs cure
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed at planting; avoid high-nitrogen feed later, which delays bulbing
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; remove any flower stems that form, as this diverts energy from the bulb

GK-ONI-001.notes: Stop watering once the foliage starts to yellow and flop — this signals the bulbs are ready to cure and dry.
GK-ONI-001.medium: Peat-free compost improved with garden compost for containers, though onions do best in open ground.

seasonalActions.Spring: Plant sets once soil is workable.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells and weed regularly — onions dislike competition.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Lift and cure bulbs once foliage yellows and flops over.
seasonalActions.Winter: Ground is generally empty; plan next year's rotation.

problems.name: Onion fly
problems.fix: Wilting, yellowing foliage with maggots at the base; rotate crops yearly and use fine mesh covers if it's a recurring problem.

problems.name: Bolting (flowering early)
problems.fix: Often triggered by a cold snap after planting; choose bolt-resistant varieties if this is a repeat issue.

propagation.season: Grown from small bulb 'sets' rather than seed for the easiest, most reliable results.
propagation.methods: Seed-grown onions are possible but need an earlier, longer indoor start.
propagation.tips: Sets are the better choice for testers wanting a simple first crop.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Onion flowers, if allowed to bolt, are visited by bees and hoverflies, though most crops are lifted before flowering.

timeline.Pruning: Remove any flower stems that form

GK-ONI-001.weeklyFact: Onion sets are just small, partially grown onions — starting from a set rather than seed gives a big head start on the season.
GK-ONI-001.pollinatorFact: If an onion is allowed to bolt and flower, its round flower head is genuinely popular with bees and hoverflies, though most crops are harvested before this happens.
weeklyFacts: Onion sets are just small, partially grown onions — starting from a set rather than seed gives a big head start on the season.
Onions belong to the same plant family as garlic, leeks and chives — the alliums.

GK-BEA-001.name: Runner Bean
GK-BEA-001.subtitle: Vigorous, productive climber with bright flowers and a long harvest window.

quickCare.Water: Water deeply and regularly, especially once flowering — irregular watering causes flowers to drop before setting pods
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered from strong wind
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed at planting; switch to potash-rich feed once flowering starts
quickCare.Pruning: Pinch out growing tips once they reach the top of their support; pick pods regularly to keep production going

GK-BEA-001.notes: Pick pods regularly, even if you don't need them yet — letting pods mature and toughen on the plant slows down new flower production.
GK-BEA-001.soil: Fertile, moisture-retentive soil, improved with organic matter before planting.
GK-BEA-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added garden compost for large containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow after frost risk passes, or start indoors for an early head start.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water deeply, feed regularly and harvest pods every few days.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Clear spent plants once cropping finishes.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Flowers dropping without setting pods
problems.fix: Usually dry soil or hot, dry air at flowering time; water deeply and regularly through this stage.

problems.name: Blackfly
problems.fix: Colonies cluster on growing tips; squash by hand or wash off with water early before numbers build.

propagation.season: Sow direct after frost, or start indoors 3-4 weeks earlier in pots for a head start.
propagation.methods: Large seeds are easy to handle and germinate reliably.
propagation.tips: Soak seed overnight before sowing to speed germination.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Bright red flowers are a strong draw for bees, and good pollination directly increases pod yield.

timeline.Pruning: Pinch growing tips once at full height; pick pods every few days

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Runner Bean.

GK-BEA-001.weeklyFact: Runner beans were originally grown in Britain as an ornamental climbing flower before anyone thought to eat the pods.
GK-BEA-001.pollinatorFact: Runner bean's bright red flowers are highly attractive to bees, and better pollination directly translates into a bigger pod harvest.
weeklyFacts: Runner beans were originally grown in Britain as an ornamental climbing flower before anyone thought to eat the pods.
A single well-grown runner bean plant can produce well over a hundred pods across a season if picked regularly.

GK-PEA-001.name: Pea
GK-PEA-001.subtitle: Sweet, early-season crop that's hard to beat picked straight from the plant.

quickCare.Water: Keep soil moist, especially once flowering and pods are forming
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed at planting; avoid high-nitrogen feed, which favours leaves over pods
quickCare.Pruning: Provide support/netting for climbing types to scramble up; no other pruning needed

GK-PEA-001.notes: Sow a new short row every couple of weeks in spring for a longer harvest window instead of one big glut.
GK-PEA-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added garden compost for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow direct as soon as soil is workable; provide support early.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently as pods form; harvest regularly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Clear spent plants; they fix nitrogen, so leave roots in the soil to break down.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late in the season
problems.fix: Water at the base and improve airflow; less of a problem with early sowings.

problems.name: Pea moth
problems.fix: Small maggots inside pods from mid-summer sowings; sowing earlier or later in the season can avoid the main egg-laying window.

propagation.season: Sow direct where they are to grow; peas dislike root disturbance from transplanting.
propagation.methods: Soak seed overnight to speed up germination.
propagation.tips: A satisfying, fast-germinating first crop for new growers.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Pea flowers are mostly self-pollinating, but bees still visit and can improve pod set in some varieties.

timeline.Pruning: Provide support as soon as tendrils appear

GK-PEA-001.weeklyFact: Garden peas are largely self-pollinating before the flower even opens, which is part of why they were one of Gregor Mendel's key plants for founding modern genetics.
GK-PEA-001.pollinatorFact: Peas are mostly self-pollinating, but bees do visit the flowers and can improve pod set and yield in some varieties.
weeklyFacts: Garden peas are largely self-pollinating before the flower even opens, which is part of why they were one of Gregor Mendel's key plants for founding modern genetics.
Frozen peas are picked and frozen within hours of harvest, which is part of why they're often sweeter than 'fresh' peas that have sat for days.

GK-CUC-001.name: Cucumber
GK-CUC-001.subtitle: Fast, vining summer crop for a sunny spot with room to sprawl or climb.

quickCare.Water: Water deeply and consistently — irregular watering causes bitter-tasting fruit
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered from strong wind
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly feed once fruiting starts
quickCare.Pruning: Train onto supports if climbing types; pinch growing tips to encourage side shoots on sprawling types

GK-CUC-001.notes: Consistent watering is the fix for bitter cucumbers — irregular dry spells are the most common cause.
GK-CUC-001.medium: Peat-free compost enriched with garden compost or manure-based improver.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in warmth; harden off before planting out.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water deeply, feed weekly and harvest regularly to keep production going.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Clear spent plants after final harvest.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Very common late in the season; water at the base, improve airflow and remove badly affected leaves.

problems.name: Bitter fruit
problems.fix: Usually from inconsistent watering or stress; water more evenly through the growing season.

propagation.season: Sow indoors in individual pots in warmth for the best germination rates.
propagation.methods: Cucumbers dislike root disturbance, so avoid unnecessary transplanting once potted.
propagation.tips: Harden off gradually before planting outdoors.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Yellow flowers attract bees, and for many outdoor varieties good pollination is essential for fruit to form at all.

timeline.Pruning: Pinch growing tips on sprawling types once established

GK-CUC-001.weeklyFact: Outdoor 'ridge' cucumbers are bred to be tougher and more weather-resistant than the smooth greenhouse types found in supermarkets.
GK-CUC-001.pollinatorFact: Many outdoor cucumber varieties need bee pollination to set fruit at all, making them one of the more pollinator-dependent crops in a typical vegetable plot.
weeklyFacts: Outdoor 'ridge' cucumbers are bred to be tougher and more weather-resistant than the smooth greenhouse types found in supermarkets.
Cucumbers are technically a fruit, botanically speaking, since they develop from a flower and contain seeds.

GK-BAS-001.name: Basil
GK-BAS-001.subtitle: Fragrant kitchen herb that rewards regular picking with bushier growth.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently; avoid letting compost dry out completely or sit waterlogged
quickCare.Light: Bright light, some direct sun; a warm windowsill suits it well
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed occasionally; too rich a feed reduces flavour concentration
quickCare.Pruning: Pinch out flower buds and growing tips regularly to keep it bushy and productive

GK-BAS-001.notes: Pinching out the growing tips regularly is the single best thing you can do — it keeps the plant bushy and delays it running to flower.
GK-BAS-001.medium: Peat-free compost; avoid waterlogging, which basil dislikes intensely.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in warmth; basil resents cold, so don't rush it outdoors.
seasonalActions.Summer: Pinch regularly, harvest leaves often and keep well watered.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Bring pots indoors before nights turn cold — basil is very frost- and cold-sensitive.
seasonalActions.Winter: Struggles outdoors; best treated as a windowsill herb through winter.

problems.name: Leaves turning black/wilting in cold
problems.fix: Basil is very cold-sensitive; bring indoors well before the first cold nights, not just frost.

problems.name: Bolting to flower quickly
problems.fix: Usually heat or root stress; pinch out flower buds promptly and keep watering consistent.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root readily in a glass of water within a couple of weeks.
propagation.methods: Sow fresh seed regularly for a continuous supply, since basil is short-lived.
propagation.tips: Warmth is essential for good germination — a windowsill or propagator works well.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Left to flower, basil's small flowers attract bees and hoverflies, though most is harvested well before flowering for the best flavour.

timeline.Pruning: Pinch tips and flower buds continually through the growing season

gallery: Flower spike if left to bolt

GK-BAS-001.weeklyFact: Basil is famously cold-sensitive — even a cool draught, let alone frost, can cause leaves to blacken and collapse.
GK-BAS-001.pollinatorFact: If allowed to flower, basil's small white or purple flower spikes are genuinely attractive to bees and hoverflies, though most is picked before flowering for the best flavour.
weeklyFacts: Basil is famously cold-sensitive — even a cool draught, let alone frost, can cause leaves to blacken and collapse.
Ancient Greeks associated basil with hatred, while in India the related holy basil (tulsi) is considered sacred — quite the reputation split.

GK-MIN-001.name: Mint
GK-MIN-001.subtitle: Vigorous, fast-spreading herb — best kept contained in its own pot.

quickCare.Water: Likes consistently moist soil — mint is thirstier than most herbs
quickCare.Light: Sun to part shade; tolerates more shade than many herbs
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed occasionally; naturally vigorous without much help
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back hard through the season to keep it productive and bushy

GK-MIN-001.notes: Grow mint in its own pot, even if it's going in the ground nearby — its spreading roots will take over a bed otherwise.
GK-MIN-001.medium: Peat-free compost; keep consistently moist, more so than most other herbs.

seasonalActions.Spring: Fresh growth appears fast; cut back hard to encourage bushy new shoots.
seasonalActions.Summer: Harvest regularly; cut back hard again if it gets leggy or starts flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back before winter; it will regrow readily next spring.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dies back to the roots in cold gardens; fully hardy and returns reliably.

problems.name: Rust (orange spots on leaves)
problems.fix: Remove and destroy affected leaves; avoid overhead watering and improve airflow.

problems.name: Invasive spreading
problems.fix: The main 'problem' with mint — always grow in a container, even when planted within a border.

propagation.season: Extremely easy from stem cuttings, which root readily in a glass of water within days.
propagation.methods: Division of an established clump is just as reliable.
propagation.tips: One of the fastest, most forgiving herbs to propagate.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: When left to flower, mint is genuinely excellent for bees and hoverflies — one of the better herb garden pollinator plants.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from cuttings or young plants
timeline.Pruning: Cut back hard several times through the growing season

gallery: Flower spike if left to bloom

GK-MIN-001.weeklyFact: Mint spreads through underground runners so vigorously that gardening advice for centuries has boiled down to one line: keep it in a pot.
GK-MIN-001.pollinatorFact: Mint left to flower is a genuinely strong pollinator plant — its small flower spikes are popular with bees and hoverflies, making it worth leaving a patch unpicked.
weeklyFacts: Mint spreads through underground runners so vigorously that gardening advice for centuries has boiled down to one line: keep it in a pot.
In Greek mythology, mint takes its name from the nymph Minthe, said to have been transformed into the plant.

GK-RSM-001.name: Rosemary
GK-RSM-001.subtitle: Woody, drought-tolerant Mediterranean herb, useful year-round.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water sparingly and let soil dry out between waterings
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed — rosemary prefers leaner soil
quickCare.Pruning: Trim after flowering to keep a good shape; avoid cutting hard into old bare wood, which won't regrow

GK-RSM-001.notes: Avoid cutting back hard into old woody stems — unlike many herbs, rosemary won't reliably regrow from bare wood.
GK-RSM-001.soil: Free-draining, slightly poor soil; avoid rich, moisture-retentive ground.
GK-RSM-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for sharp drainage, especially in pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Trim lightly to shape after any spring flowering.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in prolonged dry spells only; otherwise leave alone.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Take semi-ripe cuttings; trim lightly if needed.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen and hardy; protect pots from waterlogging in wet winters.

problems.name: Root rot in wet winters
problems.fix: Almost always from poor drainage; raise pots off the ground and ensure sharp drainage.

problems.name: No regrowth after hard pruning
problems.fix: Rosemary doesn't reliably resprout from old bare wood; always prune into leafy growth, not old stems.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer root reliably in a gritty, free-draining mix.
propagation.methods: Avoid overly humid, sealed propagation conditions, which encourage rot in woody Mediterranean herbs.
propagation.tips: A slower project than soft herbs like mint or basil, but reliable with patience.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An excellent early-season bee plant — flowers can appear as early as late winter when little else is in bloom.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from cuttings or young plants
timeline.Flowering: Late winter to spring, sometimes sporadically year-round
timeline.Pruning: Trim to shape after flowering

GK-RSM-001.weeklyFact: Rosemary can flower as early as February in mild spells, making it one of the first useful nectar sources of the year for bees emerging from winter.
GK-RSM-001.pollinatorFact: Rosemary is a genuinely valuable early-season plant for bees, sometimes flowering as early as late winter when very few other nectar sources are available.
weeklyFacts: Rosemary can flower as early as February in mild spells, making it one of the first useful nectar sources of the year for bees emerging from winter.
Rosemary's Latin name, Salvia rosmarinus, literally translates to 'dew of the sea,' from its native habitat along Mediterranean coastlines.

GK-THY-001.name: Thyme
GK-THY-001.subtitle: Low, drought-tolerant herb that doubles as excellent ground cover for bees.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water sparingly, especially in free-draining soil or gravel
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding — thyme prefers lean, poor soil
quickCare.Pruning: Trim lightly after flowering to keep it compact and prevent it becoming woody

GK-THY-001.notes: Thyme genuinely prefers poor, lean soil — resist the urge to feed or improve the ground too much.
GK-THY-001.medium: Peat-free compost with plenty of added grit; excellent drainage is essential.

seasonalActions.Spring: Trim lightly to encourage compact new growth.
seasonalActions.Summer: Let it flower — this is peak value for bees; trim lightly after flowering finishes.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Light trim if needed; avoid cutting hard this late in the season.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen and hardy in most gardens; needs no protection.

problems.name: Woody, sparse growth over time
problems.fix: Natural with age; trim lightly and regularly from early on to delay this, and be prepared to replace old plants every few years.

problems.name: Root rot in heavy or wet soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage significantly or grow in a raised bed, pot or gravel garden instead.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings root well in a gritty mix in late spring or early summer.
propagation.methods: Division of established clumps is also reliable.
propagation.tips: Avoid high humidity when propagating this Mediterranean herb, to reduce rot risk.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Low, mat-forming thyme in flower is one of the most heavily visited plants for bees in a garden, often audibly buzzing at peak bloom.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from cuttings or young plants
timeline.Pruning: Light trim after flowering

gallery: Whole plant or mat showing habit
Flower close-up with visiting insects if captured

GK-THY-001.weeklyFact: A single mature clump of flowering thyme can be visited by dozens of different bee species in a single afternoon — it's one of the best small-space plants for pollinators.
GK-THY-001.pollinatorFact: Flowering thyme is a genuine pollinator hotspot — its low, mat-forming flowers are extremely popular with a wide range of bee species and are worth leaving unpicked at peak bloom.
weeklyFacts: A single mature clump of flowering thyme can be visited by dozens of different bee species in a single afternoon — it's one of the best small-space plants for pollinators.
Ancient Greek soldiers were sometimes said to bathe in thyme-infused water before battle, believing it gave courage.

GK-SAG-001.name: Sage
GK-SAG-001.subtitle: Soft, silvery-leaved herb with strong flavour and good winter structure.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water sparingly and avoid waterlogged soil
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed — lean soil keeps flavour concentrated
quickCare.Pruning: Trim after flowering to keep a good shape; cut back lightly in spring to remove winter-damaged growth

GK-SAG-001.notes: Renew plants every few years — sage gets woody and less productive with age, and cuttings root easily to replace it.
GK-SAG-001.soil: Free-draining soil; avoid heavy, wet ground.
GK-SAG-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for sharp drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut back lightly to remove any winter-damaged growth.
seasonalActions.Summer: Let it flower if you have the space — bees love it; trim after flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Light trim if needed; avoid heavy pruning this late in the season.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen in most winters; protect pots from prolonged waterlogging.

problems.name: Woody, leggy growth with age
problems.fix: Natural over a few years; take cuttings to propagate a younger replacement plant.

problems.name: Root rot in wet soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage significantly, or grow in a raised bed or pot instead.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Layering a low stem into the soil is another simple, reliable method.
propagation.tips: Avoid high humidity when propagating, to reduce rot risk in this Mediterranean herb.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Sage's tubular purple-blue flowers are excellent for bees, particularly bumblebees suited to the flower shape.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from cuttings or young plants
timeline.Pruning: Trim after flowering; light spring tidy

gallery: Flower spike close-up if in bloom

GK-SAG-001.weeklyFact: Culinary sage is in the same genus as ornamental salvias — the tubular purple flowers, when the plant is left to bloom, look strikingly similar.
GK-SAG-001.pollinatorFact: Sage's tubular flowers, when left to bloom rather than harvested early, are a strong draw for bumblebees particularly well suited to the flower's shape.
weeklyFacts: Culinary sage is in the same genus as ornamental salvias — the tubular purple flowers, when the plant is left to bloom, look strikingly similar.
Sage's Latin name, Salvia, comes from 'salvere' — 'to be saved' or 'to heal' — reflecting its long history in traditional medicine.

GK-FOX-001.name: Foxglove
GK-FOX-001.subtitle: Tall spires of tubular flowers, brilliant for bees and woodland-edge borders.

quickCare.Water: Water in dry spells, especially in the first year while establishing
quickCare.Light: Part shade to dappled sun; tolerates more shade than many flowering plants
quickCare.Feeding: Little feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flower spikes to the base; leave some to self-seed if wanted

GK-FOX-001.notes: All parts are toxic if eaten — worth knowing if pets or young children use the garden, though it's a normal, common cottage garden plant grown safely in countless gardens.
GK-FOX-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould or organic matter.

seasonalActions.Spring: First-year plants form a leaf rosette; second-year plants send up flower spikes.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead or leave spikes to set seed for next year's plants.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Foliage may persist through mild autumns.
seasonalActions.Winter: First-year rosettes are hardy and need no protection.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: More common in dry, still conditions; improve airflow and water at the base.

problems.name: Leaf spot
problems.fix: Usually cosmetic; remove badly affected leaves and improve airflow.

propagation.season: Self-seeds readily — the easiest method is simply to let a few spikes go to seed.
propagation.methods: Sow fresh seed in late spring or summer for flowering the following year.
propagation.tips: Biennial life cycle means no flowers in the first year; that's normal, not a fault.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: One of the best plants for long-tongued bumblebees, whose bodies fit almost perfectly into the tubular flowers.

timeline.Pruning: Cut spikes to base after flowering

gallery: Whole plant showing height in a border

GK-FOX-001.weeklyFact: Foxglove's Latin name Digitalis means 'finger-like,' describing how easily a flower slips over a fingertip.
weeklyFacts: Foxglove's Latin name Digitalis means 'finger-like,' describing how easily a flower slips over a fingertip.
The heart medication digoxin was originally derived from foxglove, discovered after a folk remedy for 'dropsy' turned out to have real pharmacological effects.
GK-FOX-001.pollinatorFact: Foxglove's tubular flowers are shaped almost perfectly for bumblebees, which climb right inside to reach the nectar and pollen.

GK-GER-001.name: Hardy Geranium
GK-GER-001.subtitle: Reliable, long-flowering ground cover for borders — not to be confused with pelargoniums.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled in most varieties
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade depending on variety
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed; not a hungry plant
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back hard after first flush ('Chelsea chop' style) to encourage reflowering

GK-GER-001.notes: Often confused with pelargoniums (also called 'geraniums' in shops) — true hardy geraniums are the fully frost-hardy border perennial, not the tender bedding plant.
GK-GER-001.medium: Peat-free compost, not fussy about richness.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth flushes fast; divide congested clumps if needed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Cut back hard after the first flowering flush for a strong second bloom.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Tidy fading foliage; some varieties offer good autumn leaf colour.
seasonalActions.Winter: Mostly dies back to a low mound; fully hardy in most gardens.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Cut back hard after flowering to remove affected growth and encourage fresh, clean foliage.

problems.name: Sprawling, untidy growth
problems.fix: Cut back hard mid-season rather than letting it flop — it responds by regrowing compact and reflowering.

propagation.season: Divide established clumps in spring or autumn — quick and reliable.
propagation.methods: Basal cuttings can be taken in spring for varieties that don't divide as easily.
propagation.tips: One of the easier perennials to propagate and share between borders.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An excellent, long-flowering source of nectar and pollen for bees over a genuinely long summer season.

timeline.Flowering: May-Sep, especially with a mid-season cut back
timeline.Pruning: Hard cut-back after first flowering flush

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Hardy Geranium.

GK-GER-001.weeklyFact: Hardy geranium seed pods are shaped like a crane's bill, which is exactly where the common name 'cranesbill' comes from.
weeklyFacts: Hardy geranium seed pods are shaped like a crane's bill, which is exactly where the common name 'cranesbill' comes from.
There are over 400 species of true geranium found naturally across temperate regions worldwide, from alpine meadows to woodland edges.
GK-GER-001.pollinatorFact: Hardy geraniums flower for an unusually long season and are a dependable, easy source of nectar for bees throughout summer.

GK-VIB-001.name: Viburnum
GK-VIB-001.subtitle: Reliable evergreen shrub with winter flowers and berries for birds.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor; usually undemanding
quickCare.Pruning: Trim after flowering to shape; avoid cutting off developing berries

GK-VIB-001.notes: One of the most reliable low-maintenance evergreen shrubs for year-round structure, flowering right through winter when little else is in bloom.
GK-VIB-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Trim to shape once flowering finishes.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells, especially newly planted specimens.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Flower buds begin forming for the winter display ahead.
seasonalActions.Winter: Flowers through the coldest months — a genuinely valuable winter feature.

problems.name: Viburnum beetle
problems.fix: Larvae skeletonise leaves in spring/summer; pick off by hand on small shrubs or accept minor damage, which rarely threatens the plant.

problems.name: Bacterial leaf spot
problems.fix: Remove affected leaves and improve airflow; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably well in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Layering a low branch into the soil is a slower but reliable method.
propagation.tips: Patience is needed — evergreen shrub cuttings are generally slower than soft perennials.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Winter flowers provide a genuinely valuable nectar source for any pollinators active during mild winter spells, and the berries feed birds afterward.

timeline.Pruning: Trim after flowering, avoiding developing berries

GK-VIB-001.weeklyFact: Viburnum tinus is one of the very few shrubs that reliably flowers through the coldest months of the year in a UK or Irish garden.
weeklyFacts: Viburnum tinus is one of the very few shrubs that reliably flowers through the coldest months of the year in a UK or Irish garden.
The dark blue-black berries that follow the flowers are an important late-winter food source for garden birds when other options are scarce.
GK-VIB-001.pollinatorFact: Viburnum's winter flowers are a valuable nectar source on any mild days when pollinators are active, and its berries are an important food source for birds afterward.

GK-BOX-001.name: Boxwood
GK-BOX-001.subtitle: Classic clipped evergreen for hedging, edging and topiary.

quickCare.Water: Water well while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled, though pots need regular watering
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring and midsummer feed for hedging under regular clipping
quickCare.Pruning: Clip 1-2 times a year, typically late spring and late summer; avoid clipping in hot sun which can scorch cut edges

GK-BOX-001.notes: Box blight and box tree caterpillar have made this a higher-risk choice than it once was — inspect regularly and consider resistant alternatives like Ilex crenata for new plantings if either is present locally.
GK-BOX-001.medium: Peat-free compost for pots and topiary specimens, with good drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Clip to shape once new growth has hardened slightly.
seasonalActions.Summer: Watch closely for box tree caterpillar and box blight, both increasingly common.
seasonalActions.Autumn: A second, lighter clip if needed to tidy shape before winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen and hardy; protect topiary in pots from waterlogging.

problems.name: Box blight
problems.fix: Brown, defoliating patches in humid weather; improve airflow, remove affected growth and disinfect tools between plants.

problems.name: Box tree caterpillar
problems.fix: Rapidly strips foliage and can kill a plant in one season; inspect regularly and remove caterpillars/webbing by hand or consider resistant alternatives.

companions: Boxwood pairs with almost any formal planting

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Very slow-growing, so cuttings take a long time to reach usable size.
propagation.tips: A patience project — buying larger plants is often more practical for instant structure.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Wind-pollinated with small, inconspicuous flowers offering limited value to typical garden pollinators.

timeline.Pruning: Clip late spring and/or late summer

GK-BOX-001.weeklyFact: Traditional box parterres and topiary date back to Roman gardens, and some historic European box hedges are estimated to be centuries old.
weeklyFacts: Traditional box parterres and topiary date back to Roman gardens, and some historic European box hedges are estimated to be centuries old.
Box tree caterpillar, a relatively recent arrival from East Asia, has become one of the most serious threats to garden box in the UK and Ireland over the past decade.
GK-BOX-001.pollinatorFact: Box flowers are small and wind-pollinated, offering limited nectar value; its main garden role is structural rather than pollinator support.

GK-STR-001.name: Strawberry
GK-STR-001.subtitle: Sweet, productive fruit for beds, pots and hanging baskets alike.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently, especially as fruit swells — irregular watering causes small or split fruit
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the sweetest fruit
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed at planting; switch to potash-rich tomato-style feed once flowering
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; trim off runners unless propagating, and remove old leaves after fruiting

GK-STR-001.notes: Raise fruit off the soil with straw or matting once it starts to swell — this is the single best thing you can do to prevent slug damage and grey mould.
GK-STR-001.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil, slightly acidic if possible.
GK-STR-001.medium: Peat-free compost, ideally in a container or growing bag with good drainage for the cleanest fruit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Remove old winter-damaged leaves; feed as new growth starts.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently, straw/mat under developing fruit, and net against birds.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back foliage after fruiting; peg down runners if propagating.
seasonalActions.Winter: Mostly dormant; straw mulch protects the crown in colder gardens.

problems.name: Grey mould (botrytis) on fruit
problems.fix: Worse in wet weather or fruit touching damp soil; raise fruit off the ground and improve airflow between plants.

problems.name: Birds and slugs taking ripe fruit
problems.fix: Net against birds and use barriers or torchlight removal for slugs, especially once fruit starts to colour.

propagation.season: Runners (long trailing stems with baby plantlets) are the easiest method — peg a plantlet into a small pot while still attached, then sever once rooted.
propagation.methods: Discard very old, tired plants after 3-4 years and replace with fresh runners.
propagation.tips: One of the most beginner-friendly fruits to propagate, since it does most of the work itself.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open flowers are excellent for bees, and good pollination directly improves fruit size and shape.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from runners or young plants
timeline.Harvest: Jun-Jul, sometimes with a smaller autumn crop
timeline.Pruning: Remove old leaves after fruiting; trim runners

gallery: Whole plant showing fruit and foliage

GK-STR-001.weeklyFact: A strawberry's tiny seeds sit on the outside of the fruit, making it the only common fruit to wear its seeds like that.
weeklyFacts: A strawberry's tiny seeds sit on the outside of the fruit, making it the only common fruit to wear its seeds like that.
Poor pollination is a common cause of oddly lumpy, misshapen strawberries — each 'seed' on the surface needs individual pollination for the fruit to swell evenly around it.
GK-STR-001.pollinatorFact: Strawberry flowers rely heavily on bees for good pollination, and poorly pollinated flowers are the main cause of small or misshapen fruit.

GK-BEE-001.name: Beetroot
GK-BEE-001.subtitle: Easy, colourful root crop that also gives you edible leaves.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently — irregular watering causes woody, tough roots
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed at sowing; avoid excess nitrogen, which favours leaves over roots
quickCare.Pruning: Thin seedlings early; harvest leaves sparingly without stripping the whole plant

GK-BEE-001.notes: Each beetroot 'seed' is actually a small cluster of several seeds, so thinning is usually needed even from a sparse-looking sowing.
GK-BEE-001.soil: Free-draining, fertile soil, ideally not recently manured.
GK-BEE-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers; avoid overly rich, freshly manured soil.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow direct once soil is workable, thin seedlings as they emerge.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and harvest young roots as needed.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Main harvest window; lift before hard frost.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted roots in a cool, frost-free place if not left in the ground.

problems.name: Woody, tough roots
problems.fix: Usually from inconsistent watering or leaving roots too long before harvest; water evenly and pick at a reasonable size.

problems.name: Bolting (flowering early)
problems.fix: Triggered by cold snaps after sowing or transplant stress; sow slightly later or choose bolt-resistant varieties.

propagation.season: Sow direct in small clusters, thinning to the strongest seedling per cluster.
propagation.methods: Successive sowings every few weeks give a longer harvest window.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest and most forgiving root crops for a first-time grower.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: If allowed to bolt and flower, beetroot's small greenish flowers attract some pollinators, though most crops are harvested well before this stage.

timeline.Pruning: Thin seedlings early in the season

gallery: Root at harvest, cut open to show rings

GK-BEE-001.weeklyFact: Beetroot's deep red colour comes from betalain pigments, the same family of compounds partly responsible for the reddish-purple colour of some pokeweed and bougainvillea species.
weeklyFacts: Beetroot's deep red colour comes from betalain pigments, the same family of compounds partly responsible for the reddish-purple colour of some pokeweed and bougainvillea species.
Sugar beet, a close relative of garden beetroot, is a major source of table sugar in Europe, alongside sugar cane.
GK-BEE-001.pollinatorFact: If a beetroot bolts and flowers, its small clustered flowers can attract some pollinators, though most crops are harvested long before flowering occurs.

GK-KAL-001.name: Kale
GK-KAL-001.subtitle: Tough, productive winter green that improves after a frost.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently while establishing; fairly drought-tolerant once mature
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Feed at planting; a midsummer top-up feed helps sustain winter cropping
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; pick outer leaves regularly to encourage continued production

GK-KAL-001.notes: Flavour genuinely improves after the first frost — the cold triggers the plant to convert starches to sugars, softening the bitterness.
GK-KAL-001.soil: Fertile, firm soil, ideally not freshly dug.
GK-KAL-001.medium: Peat-free compost enriched with garden compost for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow or transplant young plants; net against pigeons and butterflies from the start.
seasonalActions.Summer: Keep well watered as plants establish through summer heat.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Main harvest period begins as leaves mature and cold weather improves flavour.
seasonalActions.Winter: Harvest through the coldest months — one of the most reliable winter crops.

problems.name: Cabbage white caterpillars
problems.fix: Net plants from planting to prevent egg-laying, since kale is a preferred host for both cabbage white species.

problems.name: Pigeon damage
problems.fix: Netting is essential in many gardens — pigeons can strip a kale bed bare in a single visit.

propagation.season: Sow direct or in modules from late spring for a winter crop.
propagation.methods: Successive sowings extend the harvest window into different stages.
propagation.tips: Reliable and forgiving from seed, one of the easier brassicas for a first attempt.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: If left to bolt in its second year, kale's yellow flowers attract bees and hoverflies, though it's usually harvested well before this stage.

timeline.Flowering: Only if left to bolt in year two
timeline.Pruning: Pick outer leaves regularly through the harvest season

GK-KAL-001.weeklyFact: Kale is in the same species as cabbage, broccoli and Brussels sprouts — all bred from the same wild Mediterranean brassica, just selected for very different traits.
weeklyFacts: Kale is in the same species as cabbage, broccoli and Brussels sprouts — all bred from the same wild Mediterranean brassica, just selected for very different traits.
A hard frost doesn't damage kale the way it does more tender crops — it actually improves the flavour by converting stored starches into sugars.
GK-KAL-001.pollinatorFact: If kale bolts and flowers in its second year, its yellow flowers are visited readily by bees and hoverflies, though most plants are harvested as a leaf crop before this happens.

GK-CHI-001.name: Chives
GK-CHI-001.subtitle: Easy, edible, clump-forming herb with pretty purple pollinator-friendly flowers.

quickCare.Water: Water regularly; likes consistently moist soil more than most Mediterranean herbs
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in spring
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back hard after flowering to encourage fresh, tender new growth

GK-CHI-001.notes: Let some flowers open before cutting them all back — the purple pompom flowers are genuinely one of the better herb-garden plants for bees.
GK-CHI-001.medium: Peat-free compost; more tolerant of regular watering than rosemary or thyme.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth emerges quickly; divide congested clumps if needed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Let some flowers open for pollinators before cutting back for a fresh flush of leaves.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back again if growth continues; otherwise leave to die back naturally.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dies back to the ground in cold gardens; fully hardy and reliable.

problems.name: Rust (orange spots on leaves)
problems.fix: Remove and destroy affected leaves; avoid overhead watering and improve airflow.

problems.name: Clump becoming congested and flowering less
problems.fix: Divide every few years in spring to keep it vigorous.

propagation.season: Division of established clumps in spring is the easiest and most reliable method.
propagation.methods: Sow seed in spring for a slower but simple start from scratch.
propagation.tips: A very forgiving herb for propagation — hard to get wrong.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Chives' purple pompom flowers are genuinely excellent for bees and are one of the better pollinator plants in a typical herb garden.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back hard after flowering finishes

GK-CHI-001.weeklyFact: Chives are the smallest species in the onion family that's widely cultivated, and unlike most alliums they're grown as much for the leaves as anything else.
weeklyFacts: Chives are the smallest species in the onion family that's widely cultivated, and unlike most alliums they're grown as much for the leaves as anything else.
The purple pompom chive flower is actually made up of dozens of tiny individual star-shaped flowers clustered together.
GK-CHI-001.pollinatorFact: Chive flowers are a genuinely strong draw for bees, and leaving a few clumps to flower before cutting back is a simple way to support pollinators in a working herb garden.

GK-CORI-001.name: Coriander
GK-CORI-001.subtitle: Fast-growing, quick-to-bolt herb best sown often in small batches.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently; drying out speeds up bolting
quickCare.Light: Light shade in hot weather; full sun in cooler conditions
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed only; too rich encourages bolting over leaf growth
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; harvest leaves regularly and pull plants once they bolt

GK-CORI-001.notes: Bolts (runs to flower) very readily in hot or dry conditions — sow small batches every 2-3 weeks rather than expecting one sowing to last all summer.
GK-CORI-001.medium: Peat-free compost; avoid letting it dry out, which triggers bolting.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow small batches every couple of weeks as soon as the soil is workable.
seasonalActions.Summer: Provide light shade in the hottest part of the day to slow bolting.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Sow again for a fresh late-season crop in cooler weather.
seasonalActions.Winter: Struggles outdoors in cold; treat as a windowsill herb through winter.

problems.name: Bolting (running to flower)
problems.fix: The main coriander problem; sow small amounts often, keep watered and provide light shade in hot weather rather than fighting a single sowing.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Sow direct where it is to grow; coriander dislikes root disturbance from transplanting.
propagation.methods: Successive small sowings are far more reliable than trying to keep one batch going.
propagation.tips: Save seed (coriander seed itself, used as a spice) from a few plants left to bolt and set seed.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Left to bolt and flower, coriander's small white-pink flower umbels are popular with hoverflies and small bees.

timeline.Flowering: If left to bolt (usually undesirable for leaf harvest)
timeline.Pruning: N/A

gallery: Flower umbel if left to bolt
Seed heads if left to set seed

GK-CORI-001.weeklyFact: Coriander leaf and coriander seed (used whole or ground as a spice) come from exactly the same plant, harvested at different stages.
weeklyFacts: Coriander leaf and coriander seed (used whole or ground as a spice) come from exactly the same plant, harvested at different stages.
A genetic variation affects whether some people perceive coriander leaf as tasting fresh and citrusy or distinctly soapy — it's a real, documented taste-perception difference, not just a preference.
GK-CORI-001.pollinatorFact: If allowed to bolt and flower, coriander's small flower umbels are genuinely useful for hoverflies and small bees, even though most plants are picked as leaves before reaching this stage.

GK-CHIL-001.name: Chilli Pepper
GK-CHIL-001.subtitle: Warmth-loving fruiting plant that rewards a long, hot growing season.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently; avoid letting compost dry out fully or sit waterlogged
quickCare.Light: Full sun, as much warmth and light as possible
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly high-potash feed once flowering and fruiting starts
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; pinch the growing tip early to encourage a bushier plant

GK-CHIL-001.notes: Needs a long, warm season to ripen well — starting seed early indoors in late winter makes a real difference in cooler climates.
GK-CHIL-001.medium: Peat-free compost, ideally with added perlite for good drainage in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in warmth as early as January-February for the longest possible season.
seasonalActions.Summer: Move outside or into a greenhouse once nights are reliably warm; feed weekly once fruiting.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Bring pots indoors before the first cold nights to keep ripening fruit going as long as possible.
seasonalActions.Winter: Struggles outdoors; treat as a houseplant/windowsill crop through winter if keeping a plant going.

problems.name: Flowers dropping without setting fruit
problems.fix: Often low humidity, heat stress, or lack of pollinating insects indoors; mist occasionally and gently shake flowering stems to help self-pollination.

problems.name: Aphids
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; check the undersides of leaves regularly, as chillies are a common target.

propagation.season: Sow seed indoors in warmth — bottom heat from a propagator speeds and improves germination significantly.
propagation.methods: Chilli seed can be slow and uneven to germinate; patience is normal, not a sign of failure.
propagation.tips: Harden off gradually before moving plants outside for the summer.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Chilli flowers are self-pollinating but still benefit from insect visits or gentle hand pollination, especially in a greenhouse or indoors.

timeline.Harvest: Jul-Oct, or until first cold nights
timeline.Pruning: Pinch growing tip early to encourage bushiness

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Chilli Pepper.

GK-CHIL-001.weeklyFact: A chilli's heat is measured in Scoville Heat Units, a scale created in 1912 by pharmacist Wilbur Scoville using a panel of tasters rather than any lab equipment.
weeklyFacts: A chilli's heat is measured in Scoville Heat Units, a scale created in 1912 by pharmacist Wilbur Scoville using a panel of tasters rather than any lab equipment.
The same chilli plant can produce fruit that ripens from green through to red, yellow or purple depending on variety, all perfectly edible at any stage.
GK-CHIL-001.pollinatorFact: Chilli flowers are self-pollinating but set fruit more reliably with help from visiting insects or a gentle shake to release pollen, especially when grown under cover.
